application_id: 11194986
activity_code: DP1
title: High-throughput identification and characterization of conserved regulators of drug-induced plasticity using single-neuron resolution atlases of the complete Caenorhabditis elegans nervous system
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Project Narrative
Chronic exposure to drugs such as psychostimulants produces long-lasting molecular
and functional changes in the brain, which in vulnerable individuals result in behavioral
changes that further predispose them to loss of control in their drug use and
progression of Substance Use Disorder (SUD). These drugs produce remarkably similar
behavioral outputs and act on similar molecular targets in the nervous system (brain
equivalent) of the simple yet powerful model organism Caenorhabditis elegans. In this
proposal, we leverage the capabilities for high-resolution, large-scale, and rapid genetic
analysis of the entire nervous system in C. elegans to identify candidate regulators of
long-lasting drug-induced changes and open up novel avenues of therapeutic
interventions for SUD.

application_id: 11244335
activity_code: F31
title: Evolutionary conservation of amacrine cell types in the vertebrate retina
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Amacrine cells perform essential computations (e.g. motion detection, contrast sensitivity, light adaptation) and
critically shape the output of the retina in ways that remain poorly understood. This work will map amacrine cell
types across species and establish methods to target these types in multiple animal models, providing direct
avenues for characterizing their function in health and investigating their dysfunction in disease. Advancing
knowledge about amacrine cells can inform strategies for retinal repair, prosthetic development, and other
therapeutic interventions aimed at preserving or restoring vision.

application_id: 11244178
activity_code: F31
title: Neural Processing During Sequential Saccades
org_name: CARNEGIE-MELLON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Generating multiple, reliable and fast eye movements to stimuli in our environment is essential in orienting us
in the visual world, yet the communication dynamics between brain regions involved in this processing are not
fully understood. Through experimental and computational methods, this project aims to investigate the
communication between regions of the brain, specifically the superior colliculus and frontal eye fields, which
are crucial nodes in the neural circuit that mediate the production of rapid eye movements. This project will
provide fruitful insight regarding canonical mechanisms for neural representation and communication within the
brain and can be extended to other sensorimotor systems.

application_id: 11067341
activity_code: F31
title: Uncovering the Function and Protein Subcellular Localization of Bicistronic Loci in Auxenochlorella protothecoides
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Project Narrative
Polycistronic genes, previously believed to be uncommon in eukaryotes, were discovered to be widespread in
the green algal lineage, with many of these genes encoding both pioneer proteins and proteins predicted to be
widely conserved in the eukaryotic lineage. In previous work, I sought to understand how the downstream ORF
is translated in eukaryotes in a bicistronic context, and I pinpointed leaky scanning by the ribosome in
combination with Kozak sequence strength as the underlying mechanism. Now, I will deduce the function of
highly conserved genes in such bicistronic transcripts using a “model” organism suited for reverse genetics by
generating loss of function mutant strains and determining the subcellular localization of the proteins with reporter
constructs.

application_id: 11064291
activity_code: F31
title: Quantifying adapted balance control during walking in chronic stroke
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF DELAWARE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Post-stroke balance and gait impairments compromise walking adaptability, which limits safe community
ambulation for those with chronic stroke. This proposal seeks to characterize how stroke and walking task
complexity affect the biomechanical mechanisms for controlling lateral balance during walking, as well as to
understand the interplay between walking adaptability and balance control mechanisms in individuals with
chronic stroke. This work will provide an understanding of how improvements in complex walking performance
may correspond with adapted balance control, minimizing a critical barrier to safe walking activity in the free-
living environment for those with chronic stroke.

application_id: 11242874
activity_code: F32
title: Osteopontin drives epithelial plasticity in pancreatitis
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative We have discovered that unique cell types, including tuft cells and enteroendocrine cells, emerge during pancreatic injury, with tuft cells playing a critical role in restraining metaplasia and inflammation. Through an in- depth exploration of our pancreatic injury single cell RNA-sequencing dataset, we identified a progenitor-like "uncommitted" cell population expressing osteopontin (SPP1), which is predicted to seed metaplastic lineages. Our work aims to define SPP1-mediated signaling pathways and identify the most progenitor-like populations of these cells, uncovering mechanisms to modulate cellular plasticity in chronic pancreatic injury.

application_id: 11163901
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the impact of chromosome 21 dosage on the placenta secretome and neurodevelopment
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This research proposal aims to address a key unanswered question: whether placental abnormalities contribute to altered
cortical development in individuals affected by Trisomy 21 (T21). Here, I investigate the effects of human chromosome
21 dosage (HSA21) and HSA21-linked genes APP and SOD1 on placental function, alterations in neuroactive compounds
secreted from placental syncytiotrophoblast and the resulting neurodevelopmental consequences. Our approach will
uncover the role of specific T21 genes on T21-induced oxidative stress on the placenta-brain axis in neurodevelopment,
test a pregnancy-safe nutritional supplement as a potential prenatal treatment, enable identification of novel T21 prenatal
therapeutic targets, and more broadly facilitate our understanding of the ...

application_id: 11165746
activity_code: F31
title: Trends in	Severe Maternal Morbidity resulting from Pregnancy Loss
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN
phr: Project Narrative
Poor maternal health in the United States is a cause of increasing alarm, but the research on severe maternal
morbidity does not consider morbidity experienced during or after pregnancy loss. Recent restrictive
reproductive health policies may be impacting treatment, care-seeking, and health outcomes for people
experiencing pregnancy loss- a potentially serious medical event during which adequate healthcare is essential
to health and wellbeing. This research quantifies severe morbidity experienced during pregnancy loss in Texas
and assesses changes in hospital-based pregnancy loss care-seeking and severe morbidity experienced
during pregnancy loss after restrictive reproductive health policies were implemented in Texas.

title: Medical Scientist Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposal aims to develop physician-scientists through integrating rigorous scientific investigation and compassionate clinical training through our multi-institutional MD/PhD program. We enable training of a diverse national trainee pool in a wide variety of fields from engineering and biomedical devices, systems neuroscience, immunotherapy to development of novel therapeutics and discovery of molecular basis for disease. Trainees supported by this grant will become the future biomedical physician-scientists who will work towards increasing our understanding of fundamental pathogenesis of disease and cutting-edge approaches to treat patients.

application_id: 11161862
activity_code: F31
title: Evaluating the Feasibility of Force-Based and Position-Based Prosthetic Control for Children Born without a Hand
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project Narrative
Children born with upper-limb deficiencies have high rates of prosthetic abandonment due to a
lack of functional, intuitive devices catered towards their demographic. By fusing two established
prosthetic control systems, we can define the level of muscular control in these children. We can
then leverage this understanding to develop a new model for pediatric prostheses that provides
not only more accurate grasp classification, but also the possibility of proportional force and
position control.

application_id: 11162811
activity_code: F31
title: Uncovering the role of RFT1 in growth and developmental signaling
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Mutation of the RFT1 gene causes a genetic disorder called a congenital disorder of glycosylation (RFT1-CDG)
that results in severe symptoms that present at infancy and span most organ systems, producing major defects
in growth and development. The biological function of RFT1 remains poorly understood, and hence, treatment
options for RFT1-CDG are lacking. This project seeks to study the mechanism of RFT1 in regulating the growth
and developmental signaling pathways that may contribute to the clinical presentation of the disorder and will
inform treatment options for RFT1-CDG patients.

activity_code: F31
title: Obesity-induced endometrial stromal cell fibrosis and immunosuppression leads to increased endometrial hyperplasia disease severity
org_name: HENRY FORD HEALTH + MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Obesity is a growing epidemic, with approximately 40% of reproductive-age women in the United States (US) considered obese. There are an estimated 220,000 cases of endometrial hyperplasia (EH) diagnosed each year within the US and obesity increases a woman’s risk for EH by 3-fold. The proposed studies will investigate the mechanisms by which obesity alters endometrial stromal cell composition and signaling to induce EH development and increased disease severity, which can be later manipulated to develop stroma-specific treatment targets in obese women with EH.

application_id: 11165773
activity_code: F31
title: Leveraging Digital Technologies to Advance Rehabilitation for Children with Unilateral Cerebral Palsy
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Telehealth motor intervention for children with unilateral cerebral palsy (UCP) has the potential to make
effective therapies more accessible to families who live in rural or under-resourced areas, but we need to
determine if telehealth therapies result in changes in upper extremity outcomes across the International
Classification of Function. We will compare in person and telehealth activity-level outcomes and use wrist-worn
accelerometers to measure changes in body structure/function in the real world and quantify the dose of
intervention that parents deliver during at-home practice. Results will enable future trials to use digital
technologies to collect motor outcome data and objectively measure adherence to at-home practice of UE
activities.

application_id: 10918129
activity_code: DP5
title: Regulation of Proinflammatory Cytokine Responses by a Caspase-8-N4BP1 Axis
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative Inflammation is a hallmark of many diseases, including cancer, autoinflammatory disorders, immunodeficiencies, and neurodegeneration; thus, understanding the molecular mechanisms regulating inflammation is critical to identifying opportunities for therapeutic intervention. The signaling pathway identified in this proposal represents a completely novel mechanism by which an apoptotic caspase controls inflammation via a network of proteins with both recognized and unknown roles in inflammation. Mechanistic elucidation of this pathway will shed light on fundamental biology and enable novel therapeutic strategies.

title: The association of a person-centered care intervention during childbirth with clinical and patient-reported outcomes in Washington state hospitals
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health as it leverages an ongoing intervention during childbirth
(“TeamBirth”) across 55 hospitals in Washington state to estimate its impact on patient-reported experience
measures and clinical interventions during childbirth. Achieving these research aims will provide evidence to
support the integration of TeamBirth in labor and delivery rooms across Washington State and throughout the
United States, as TeamBirth is being implemented in multiple states and has the potential to be scaled widely.
This project supports NICHD’s research priority to set the foundation for healthy pregnancies and lifelong
wellness.

application_id: 11145107
activity_code: K23
title: Using real-world evidence to define safe pain management strategies in cirrhosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pain is a highly prevalent and undertreated symptom in patients with cirrhosis, a population which may also be
particularly vulnerable to adverse outcomes related to pain medications. Currently, there is limited
understanding of how pain is managed or the actual, real-world harms associated with use of pain medications
in this population. This proposal aims to quantify the harms of specific types of pain medications in cirrhosis
patients, using these data to develop evidence-based guidance to help clinicians feel more comfortable
managing pain, improving quality of life and outcomes for patients with cirrhosis.

The proposed project targets early visual neurodevelopment to investigate how differences in
excitation/inhibition (E/I) balance may serve as a biological index of neuroplasticity that relates to emerging
neural function and cognition in humans. Non-human animal models of healthy visual neurodevelopment and
of neurodevelopmental disorders suggest that differences in early E/I balance give rise to normative individual

balance in relation to indices of human brain function and cognition is critical to providing mechanistic

application_id: 11165598
activity_code: F31
title: Word Learning and the Spatial Context of Naturalistic Scenes
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: Project Narrative
Language input to children is replete with environmental regularities, and words occurring in consistent places
are learned more easily than words that are more scattered in use. The current project examines the possibility
that word learning in easier in consistent places because learners can use the conceptual meaning of distinct
locations to help them figure out the meanings of novel words (e.g., the kitchen is a place to prepare food).
Understanding the role of non-linguistic information in word learning will support novel approaches to therapy
for children who experience word learning difficulty, including those with language disorders.

title: ActivityChoice: A clinic-delivered implementation program to increase physical activity and decrease cardiovascular disease risk amongst cancer survivors
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cardiovascular disease, the number one leading cause of death in the United States, is highly prevalent in
cancer survivors. Physical activity can reduce risk, and referrals to programs addressing survivors’ choices are
highly recommended from providers in cancer survivorship, though rarely implemented. We propose to
develop ActivityChoice, a clinic-based implementation program, using patient narrative decision aids to support
choices to a group in-person, group virtual, or self-monitored digital health physical activity program.

application_id: 11190131
activity_code: DP1
title: Role of social touch and social memory in organizing naked mole-rat colonies
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it will provide a molecular, neuronal,
and behavioral dissection of how social touch is used for encoding the memory and identities of
large numbers of conspecifics. It remains unclear how the human brain can achieve this difficult
task, and therefore, studies in a specialist animal like the naked mole-rat could provide critical
new insights. The project is relevant to the mission of NIH because of its potential to enhance our
fundamental understanding of the role of touch in shaping social interactions, which is critical for
our well-being and oftentimes disrupted in neurological and psychiatric diseases.

application_id: 11242910
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the Role of CDC42 and RAC1 in Germline Cyst Breakdown and Primordial Follicle Formation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Germline cyst breakdown and primordial follicle formation are crucial to the formation of a healthy ovarian
reserve, and thus a woman’s fertility. This proposal aims to understand how two Rho GTPases, CDC42 and
RAC1, influence the cytoskeleton of developing oocytes to form healthy and long-lasting primordial follicles.
Upon completion of this project, we will have a better understanding of how germline cyst breakdown and
primordial follicle formation occur, helping to understand an important aspect of women’s reproductive health.

application_id: 11178428
activity_code: K23
title: Does social motivation in adolescence differentially predict the impact of childhood threat exposure on developing suicidal thoughts and behaviors
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Childhood threat exposure (e.g., family/community violence) is a key risk factor for developing suicidal
thoughts and behaviors; yet, not all adolescents with childhood threat exposure develop these symptoms.
Disruptions to social motivation – the motivations driving the desire and effort to socially interact – may play a
role in differentiating suicide risk. The current study will identify whether maladaptive social motivation
presentations during adolescence moderate the link between childhood threat exposure and suicidal
thoughts/behaviors in an effort to improve risk identification and mechanistic targets for interventions.

application_id: 11244989
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of a novel RNA binding protein during dauer in C. elegans
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: While microRNAs (miRNAs) are known essential regulators of animal development, how miRNA-mediated gene
silencing adapts to environmental stress, such as starvation, remains poorly understood. We have identified the
conserved RNA-binding protein UNK-1 and its cofactor CRI-1 as novel interactors of the microRNA-induced
silencing complex (miRISC) in the model organism C. elegans. Here, I will test the hypothesis that UNK-1 and
CRI-1 promote miRISC-mediated silencing of pro-developmental genes under stress conditions, thereby
enabling stress-adaptive gene expression programs to help animals respond effectively to environmental
challenges.

application_id: 11132831
activity_code: F30
title: Treatment of Alzheimer’s Disease using Ultrasound-Targeted Microbubble Cavitation-Mediated Blood Brain Barrier Opening to Facilitate Drug Delivery to the Brain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a devastating, progressive, neurodegenerative disease that affects millions of
Americans, yet there is no cure, and there are very limited treatment options. As the blood brain barrier (BBB)
blocks nearly all drugs from entering the central nervous system, ultrasound contrast agents activated by
ultrasound are being explored to transiently open the BBB and enhance drug delivery to the brain. This proposal
will determine the mechanisms by which ultrasound contrasts agents open the BBB and use this drug delivery
strategy to ultimately increase the number of therapeutic candidates that can be delivered to the brain for the
treatment of AD and other neurologic diseases.

application_id: 11197242
activity_code: DP1
title: Dissecting and modulating cell type and circuit-specific epigenetic mechanisms of cocaine addiction
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative:
Cocaine addiction is especially hard to treat due to the formation of stable neural circuits that detect drug-
associated cues, causing cocaine-seeking behavior and frequent relapses. We propose to study the cell-type
and limbic circuit-specific epigenetic changes that underlie long-term cocaine addiction. We will then test the
hypothesis that cocaine seeking behavior can be erased by targeting pathological circuits established by
repeated cocaine exposure to suppress their activity via circuit-specific epigenetic reprogramming.

title: Molecular and functional characterization of type I and II vestibular hair cells in adult mice
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative  Vestibular hair cells are essential for our sense of balance, equilibrium, and general well- being. Degeneration of hair cells is thought to be a primary cause of vestibular dysfunction in aging patients and in patients exposed to ototoxic drugs. This project seeks to provide important new information about the molecular signatures and functions of vestibular hair cells in mice that will contribute toward developing new therapies to restore balance function to human patients.

application_id: 11256714
activity_code: K01
title: Preclinical Cardiovascular Disease among Adolescents and Young Adults in Haiti
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARATIVE
There are nearly 5 million adolescents and young adults living with HIV (AYALH) globally who have survived
growing up with HIV and are now emerging into adulthood to confront high rates of HIV-related co-morbidities
including preclinical cardiovascular disease (CVD) which is associated with premature death and disability. The
epidemiology of preclinical CVD among AYALH is not defined, including age of onset, progression, and
associated risk factors. In this K01 proposal, Dr. Reif will conduct a prospective cohort study of 500 HIV+ and
500 HIV- participants ages 18-30 years in Haiti followed for 3 years to address this knowledge gap and inform
the adaptation of evidence-based CVD interventions for AYALH and for integration into HIV clinics.

application_id: 10891674
activity_code: K99
title: Developing a multi-component intervention to address cardiometabolic disease (CMD) risk in Samoan children
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROPOSAL NARRATIVE
Cardiometabolic disease risk is emerging earlier in life than ever before and disproportionately affects racial and
ethnic minority populations. This proposal will apply advanced methods in epidemiology and implementation
science to identify, adapt, and test a multi-component intervention to address cardiometabolic disease risk in
Samoan children. Results will inform action that can be taken at the earliest possible opportunity to mitigate
cardiometabolic disease risk and reduce health disparities among Samoans and other Pacific Islander

title: Do Acquisitions of Hospice Agencies by Private Equity Firms and Publicly Traded Corporations Impact End-of-Life Quality and Cost of Care?
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative
Concern has been raised by policymakers due to increasing private equity (PE) and publicly traded corporation (PTC)
investment in hospice agencies and their purported effects on hospice care. PE- and PTC- owned hospices are under
pressure to deliver short-term, above market returns to investors, which could have detrimental effects on hospice care.
Leveraging a mixed methods approach to study the impact of PE and PTC hospice acquisitions could have important
policy and clinical implications.

application_id: 10921550
activity_code: F31
title: m6A Modification of HIV RNA Affects its Post-transcriptional Processing
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Current latency reversing agents (LRAs) for the “shock and kill” strategy for an HIV cure do not target post- transcriptional steps of HIV replication, and thus, cannot optimally reactivate latent HIV infections. Little is known about the post-transcriptional processing of HIV transcripts and whether these processes can be targeted as a latency reversal method. The goal of the proposed study is to investigate the role of m6A modifications on HIV transcripts in the nuclear localization and trafficking of these RNA species and how modulating this modification can affect HIV RNA expression.

title: Exploring PAR2 Trafficking in Oral Cancer: Implications for Pain Signaling and Drug Delivery
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Oral cancer pain, the most common and severe form of pain associated with cancer, is ineffectively treated with opioids. The proposed studies aim to investigate how cancer influences the protease-activated receptor 2 (PAR2), a demonstrated mediator of oral cancer pain, to move inside neurons (trafficking) and promote pain signals (signaling). The proposed research training and career development programs will contribute to my long- term goal of directing an academic research laboratory devoted to developing nanomedicines that target trafficking and signaling of PAR2 and similar receptors to provide more effective relief for oral cancer pain.

application_id: 11261254
activity_code: DP2
title: Engineering synthetic cellular crosstalk for transplantation tolerance
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Acute and chronic alloimmune reactions against allografts are the major causes of transplantation
rejection. Developing safe and effective immune suppressive treatments to achieve long-term
transplantation tolerance has been difficult because we don’t have strategies to specifically
intervene with multiple components of the alloimmune reactions without compromising the entire
host immune system. Here we will develop an orthogonal vaccine to synthetically bridge the
crosstalk between antigen presenting cells and engineered regulatory T cells to simultaneously
and specifically suppress alloimmune reactions at both the effector site (allograft) and the priming
site (allograft draining lymphoid tissue) for long-term transplantation tolerance.

title: MOLECULAR ANALYSIS OF MALARIA MITOCHONDRIAL GENE REGULATION
org_name: IDAHO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: In the human malaria parasite, Plasmodium falciparum, the lack of knowledge regarding most
essential processes and regulatory pathways, particularly in the mitochondria, is a critical barrier
to identifying and developing new drug targets. Ideally these targets are highly conserved and
should be resistant to mutation or have limited ability to change in order to evolve resistance.

aspects of parasite mitochondrial biology, with the goal of advancing rational drug design
against this deadly parasite.

application_id: 11261149
activity_code: DP2
title: Partial maturation in mosquito-borne flaviviruses: developing new approaches to characterize the role of lattice heterogeneity in fusion, infectivity, and antibody neutralization
org_name: PURDUE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mosquito-borne flaviviruses present a significant global health burden, with Dengue, West Nile
and Zika viruses causing millions of infections and thousands of deaths annually. A better
understanding of the viral surface will aid structure-based pharmaceutical design efforts to
develop more effective treatments and safer vaccines. This proposal describes research to
characterize poorly understood heterogeneity in the viral surface lattice of E proteins, combining
novel technologies and analyses to improve structural models and antibody neutralization
stoichiometry predictions, and clearly establishing which lattice arrangements are most important
for infectivity.

org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 This project operates at the unexplored interface of two key regulatory layers of genome structure and
function – chromatin modification and DNA looping – with the comprehensive characterization of the functional
implications of an unexpected interaction between the histone methyltransferase SETD2 and cohesin. A
functional interplay between these factors would signify the first example of an enzyme typically operating at the

cohesin and SETD2 have important roles spanning genome organization, transcription, and splicing, leaving this
project well-positioned to provide novel insight into how their activities are regulated, and how pathogenic

application_id: 11318241
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating PHGDH deficiency inborn error metabolism in vitro and in vivo using gene regulation and metabolic tracing approaches
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative
PHGDH deficiency is a rare inborn error of metabolism causing profound neurological phenotypes, due to loss
of function in PHGDH, the rate limiting enzyme for de novo serine biosynthesis. The metabolic mechanisms
linking PHGDH loss to neurodevelopmental defects remain poorly understood due to the downstream
biochemical pathway complexities of serine metabolism. This proposal will leverage newly developed PHGDH
deficiency cell systems and mouse models, along with [13C] metabolic tracer assays, to uncover mechanisms
of PHGDH metabolic dysregulation that lead to nervous system dysfunction.

this deep learning-based pipeline to show differences in BAV patients with aortic dilatation vs healthy controls

application_id: 11302164
activity_code: DP2
title: Activity-dependent probes for spatially-defined proteomics
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
A loss or imbalance of specific proteins is thought to be associated with many brain disorders such as autism,
epilepsy, neuropsychiatric disease, and Alzheimer’s disease. This proposal aims to develop and deploy new
methodologies that would allow us to capture the protein expression of neurons whose activity is relevant for a
particular disease model. Ultimately these technologies may uncover biomarkers that can be targeted in
specific subpopulations of neurons to treat brain disorders relevant to human health.

application_id: 11071643
activity_code: F31
title: PROTECTIVE POISON AT HIGH ALTITUDE: THERAPEUTIC EFFECTS OF ELEVATED ENDOGENOUS CO FROM ADAPTIVE HMOX2 VARIANTS
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative
Pulmonary arterial hypertension is a global health concern that is characterized by pulmonary arterial wall
thickening, vasoconstriction, and thrombus. Recent investigations of populations residing at high altitude
revealed that Tibetan highlanders are protected from pulmonary arterial hypertension, possibly through
adaptations in the heme catalysis and endogenous production of carbon monoxide. This project seeks to link
single nucleotide variants to observed adaptive phenotypes and translate them to further the understanding and
use of carbon monoxide releasing molecules in treating pulmonary arterial hypertension.

activity_code: F31
title: Hypothalamic oxytocin neuron activation rescues cardiac metabolism through cardiac cholinergic signaling in type 2 diabetes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF HAWAII AT MANOA
phr: Narrative
Diabetic cardiovascular autonomic neuropathy is among the most common yet least understood complications
of type 2 diabetes (T2D) despite significant negative impact on survival and quality of life. A goal of this project
is to identify the effects of T2D on brainstem-originating neuronal communication to the heart in a model of high
fat diet and streptozotocin-induced T2D in rats. Our studies will also test the innovative hypothesis that timed
activation of a small population of hypothalamic paraventricular neurons will slow the development of, or reverse,
the deleterious autonomic and cardiac function alterations that occur during T2DM.

application_id: 11138121
activity_code: K08
title: Sex Differences in Cardiac Macrophages in Response to Pulmonary Hypertension
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative
Pulmonary arterial hypertension is a rare disease with high mortality affecting predominantly women.
However, men who develop pulmonary hypertension have higher rates of right heart failure and death than
women. We seek to understand the biologic reasons for the protection of right heart function in women by
studying sex differences in the function of immune cells of the heart, in hopes that this will lead to new
treatments to improve right heart function in pulmonary hypertension patients.

application_id: 11162612
activity_code: F31
title: The Role of Lymphatic Endothelial Alpha Globin in Heart Failure
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) affects greater than 3 million Americans and is highly
associated with cardiometabolic diseases such as obesity, type II diabetes, and hypertension. Cardiometabolic
disease-related lymphatic vessel dysfunction has been shown to cause inflammation, which promotes left
ventricular hypertrophy and diastolic dysfunction in the heart as is observed in HFpEF patients. We believe that
cardiac lymphatic vessels contribute to the progression of cardiometabolic disease-driven heart failure and that
investigating the impact of such contributions can inform future decisions related to therapeutic targeting.

title: Molecular Machines Mechanism and Structure (M3S) Training Program
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY, THE
phr: Project Narrative The goal of this training program is to train science and engineering students in the fundamental principles underlying molecular machines, the enzymes, motors, switches, and pumps that power cell function. Students will be trained in structural biology, single-molecule biophysics, and computation, and additionally will learn how to create 3D molecular simulations. Trainees will be prepared for careers in interdisciplinary molecular biophysics research, industries such as pharmaceuticals or biomedical devices, teaching, or science policy positions.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mechanical thrombectomy, while being effective in the treatment of large vessel occlusion in acute ischemic
stroke, suffers drawbacks due to procedural complications that may occur during treatment, with up to 40% of
treated patients experiencing distal embolization. Furthermore, optimizing mechanical thrombectomy procedures
is limited by the little work done to investigate how procedural outcomes for blood clots of varied geometry,
composition, and location are affected by treatment parameters such as i) stent retriever length, size, and speed
of withdrawal, or ii) aspiration catheter geometry, distance from clot, and suction pressure. The proposed project
develops a computational model of thrombectomy using experimental data from in vitro thrombectomy
procedures to dete...

application_id: 10826082
activity_code: F32
title: Dissecting the transcription factor networks controlling fetal T-lineage differentiation
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative The first wave of thymocyte differentiation in the fetal thymus proceeds rapidly and gives rise to specific lineages of innate lymphocytes and innate-like T cells that are not generated by any subsequent wave of development. Based on my preliminary data, I hypothesize that these populations arise from a dedicated precursor cell in the fetal thymus that is using a unique differentiation program. In this proposal, I aim to test test this hypothesis through fate mapping and perturbation studies to construct a full lineage hierarchy of fetal thymocyte differentiation and the gene regulatory networks that govern it.

application_id: 11066100
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanosensitive Mechanisms of Premature Calcification in Bicuspid Aortic Valve Disease
org_name: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Bicuspid aortic valve disease is the most prevalent congenital heart defect, in which 50% of
patients develop premature calcification and associated stenosis during early adult life. It’s
believed that alterations in mechanical stress on the aortic valve as a result of the congenital
malformation, may contribute early calcification; however, the mechanisms are not known. This
proposal aims to understand this and identify the signaling pathway that relays abnormal
biomechanical stress received by valve endothelial cells, to underlying valve interstitial cells to
promote a calcification program.

application_id: 11380235
activity_code: DP2
title: Novel Strategies to Enhance Drug Delivery and Tumor Immunogenicity in Pancreatic Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE
The tumor microenvironment (TME) in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is characterized by a dense
and anergic stroma that inhibits vascular perfusion, suppresses immune function, and reduces tumor
permeability to drugs and immune cells. We propose developing and evaluating a novel oncolytic virus
modified to selectively deliver stroma-degrading enzymes to enhance the expression of neoantigens and
increase tumor immunogenicity as well as immune cell infiltration. Currently, advanced PDAC patients have
limited treatment options; thus, this project will address a critical unmet need for more effective therapies.

application_id: 11069995
activity_code: F31
title: Exploring Functional Divergence in Human and Chimpanzee Arteries: Implications for Cardiovascular Disease
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 The factors that induce atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, the leading cause of death
worldwide, are not fully understood despite decades of research. Study of our closest living
relative, the chimpanzee, could provide novel insights into the mechanism of this disease as they
rarely develop atherosclerotic lesions despite having higher serum cholesterol levels. I intend to
use two newly developed methods to create arterial endothelial cells from human, chimpanzee,
and human-chimpanzee hybrid cells to investigate species-specific changes in gene expression
that may underlie differences in the pathogenesis of atherosclerosis.

title: Investigating the Heterogeneous Intercellular Signaling of Hematopoietic Stem and Progenitor Cells and its Changes around the Circadian Clock
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hematopoietic stem cells are responsible for life-long blood regeneration and, as such, are regarded as the
most important cell types for regenerative medicine. The proposed research will study how cell-cell
communication in the bone marrow regulates the production of blood and immune cells and how cell-cell
signaling changes around the circadian clock. Potential findings can help understand physiological response to
bleeding, infection, and disease, and may reveal new therapeutic targets for blood and immune disorders.

spectrometry-based proteomic approaches. The success of this work will provide a transformative understanding

application_id: 11160967
activity_code: F31
title: Unraveling the mechanisms behind lymphatic dysfunction in obesity: are weight-loss drugs effective at restoring lymphatic function?
org_name: TULANE UNIVERSITY OF LOUISIANA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Clinical studies have demonstrated that obesity increases the risk of developing lymphedema; however, the
molecular mechanisms behind obesity-driven lymphatic dysfunction remain poorly understood, leading to a lack
of treatment options. This proposal will 1) identify and characterize transcriptomic and functional alterations in
lymphatic vessels from diet-induced obese ApoE KO mice and 2) assess the effects of GLP-1R agonists (i.e.
Semaglutide) on the function of lymphatic vessels and determine the therapeutic potential of GLP-1R weight-
loss drugs to improve lymphatic function in diet-induced obese ApoE KO mice.

application_id: 11162909
activity_code: F31
title: The role of 12-lipoxygenase in regulating the effects of EPA on platelet activation and thrombosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), an omega-3 fatty acid enriched in fish oil supplements, reduces the risk for major
cardiovascular events which are highly regulated by platelet hyperreactivity. It has been shown several fatty
acids alter platelet activation through their 12-lipoxygenase (12-LOX) metabolites. This proposal seeks to
determine the mechanisms by which EPA alters platelet function and thrombus formation through its 12-LOX
metabolite, 12-HEPE, which will inform the use of supplements containing EPA in the prevention of thrombotic
cardiovascular diseases.

application_id: 11430672
activity_code: DP2
title: Sequence models of genome regulatory architecture in 3D
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
The genomic sequence directs its 3D organization and gene regulation which is essential for the normal
development and function of the human body. Genome variations across individuals may disrupt structural and
gene regulations and contribute to susceptibility to diseases and response to therapeutics, yet the
consequences of genome variations are generally unknown. Computational models proposed in this project will
decode the structural and transcriptional impact of any genome sequence and open up new possibilities in
assessing the impacts of genome variants and understanding their roles in disease genetics.

application_id: 11454382
activity_code: DP2
title: The inactive X: discovering sex genes that influence female vulnerability to Alzheimer's disease
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Women are more likely to exhibit higher levels of Alzheimer’s disease pathology than men, even at the earliest
stages of disease, however, no convincing biological mechanism has been proposed to explain this sex-
specific vulnerability to disease. I propose a novel conceptualization of the problem through the examination of
the X chromosome; specifically, X-linked genes that escape inactivation on the second X chromosome in
women. These genes are often involved with immune responses pathways – a known mechanism for greater
amyloid and tau pathological burden - and which could have major implications for female susceptibility to
Alzheimer’s disease.

title: Rod photoreceptor regeneration in a zebrafish model of Retinitis Pigmentosa.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: Retinitis Pigmentosa (RP) is a chronic retinal degenerative disease that affects about 100,000 people in the United States annually and ultimately results with patients becoming blind early on in their adulthood. This project seeks to identify the gene expression networks that control the photoreceptor regeneration process so that current therapies can directly target and successfully treat patients with RP.

application_id: 11242364
activity_code: F31
title: Dissecting the Role of Mechanical Forces in the Branching Morphogenesis of the Mouse Bronchial Tree
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Lung development requires a tight interplay between the pulmonary epithelium and its surrounding
mechanical environment, including the airway smooth muscle and the pleural cavity. The proposed research will
test the hypotheses that airway smooth muscle contractions set the pace of branching events in a pressure-
dependent manner, and that both smooth muscle contraction and branching dynamics are modulated by local
compressive forces. Upon completion, the findings from this proposal will reveal the role of airway smooth muscle
contractions in lung branching morphogenesis and help guide the creation of mechanical therapies for mitigating
fetal lung hypoplasia.

title: RNA splicing regulation of lipid metabolism and longevity in C. elegans
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Manipulation of TORC1 signaling extends lifespan and increases disease resistance but has pleiotropic effects making it critical to understand novel downstream mediators, like pre-mRNA splicing, to better target interventions. application_id: 11243700
activity_code: F31
title: The Role of Glycans in Influenza A Virus Mucosal Trapping
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mucus in the airway is designed to trap inhaled pathogens such as influenza A virus through three primary
mechanisms: glycan-pathogen trapping, steric trapping, and antibody-mediated trapping. The interdependence
between these mechanisms has yet to be fully explored, as such this project will mechanistically study mucin-
associated glycan-mediated trapping and neutralization of influenza virus human airway epithelial models to
explore. Successful completion of this proposed study will reveal new insights into IAV pathogenesis and innate
defense mechanisms against infection.

application_id: 11243064
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular and Cellular Mechanisms of the Aberrant Type I Interferon Response in VEXAS Syndrome
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
VEXAS (Vacuoles, E1-ubiquitin-activating enzyme, X-linked, Autoinflammatory, Somatic) syndrome is a newly
reported and severe hematologic-autoinflammatory syndrome afflicting an estimated ~1 in 4,269 men older than
50 years. Despite its severity and prevalence, molecular mechanisms underlying VEXAS pathogenesis remain
poorly understood owing to a lack of genetically engineered cell culture and animal models. This proposal reports
the development of novel genetically engineered models of VEXAS that will enable us to examine the
mechanisms and interplay between aberrant interferon-mediated inflammation and myeloid-biased
hematopoiesis in this disease.

application_id: 11242253
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the Impact of Load-Dependent and Structural Arterial Stiffness on Cerebrovascular Blood Flow in Individuals Post-Stroke
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Following a stroke, individuals exhibit increased arterial stiffness and reduced cerebral blood flow, which places
them at an elevated risk for cerebrovascular decline and recurrent stroke. The proposed research will
investigate how arterial stiffness mechanisms, namely load-dependent and structural arterial stiffness,
influence resting and dynamic cerebral blood flow in individuals with stroke. This work will therefore provide
critical information for the development of future research trials seeking to reduce arterial stiffness and offset
the related cerebrovascular decline post-stroke.

application_id: 11245609
activity_code: F31
title: Identifying novel microproteins and protein translation mechanisms involved in the development of atherosclerosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Atherosclerosis, which is driven by plaque buildup in the arteries, is a major cause of heart disease and has
been associated with elevated bile acid levels. We have previously discovered extrahepatic bile acid release
(under revision in Cell) and to address this result, we propose implementing ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq) and
RNA sequencing techniques to identify translationally regulated genes contributing to bile acid (BA) abnormality
in liver, muscle, kidney, and colon; all of which are tissues which we found to show high induction of BA synthesis
(Aim 1). Additionally, we will use this Ribo-seq data to identify novel microproteins relevant to the development
of atherosclerosis.

application_id: 11244304
activity_code: F31
title: Deciphering the output of fetal hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells at homeostasis and in response to inflammation
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Fetal hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) and multipotent progenitors (MPPs) are critical for the development of
the adult blood and immune systems, but how these progenitors respond to prenatal inflammation or early life
infections, and how this impacts offspring immunity remain poorly understood. We hypothesize that distinct fetal
MPP populations mediate the response to prenatal inflammation and generate immune cells that may protect
the offspring from early life-infections, while preserving the fetal HSC pool. The objective of this grant is to define
the function and cellular output of specific fetal HSCs and fetal MPPs over time, both at steady state and in
response to prenatal inflammation, and to investigate how these processes shape postnatal immune function.

application_id: 11245364
activity_code: F31
title: Multi-Axis 3D Bioprinting of Cardiac Tissues with Ventricular Helical Alignment
org_name: CARNEGIE-MELLON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Current methods for palliative repair of single-ventricle heart malformations often lead to life-long comorbidities
associated with the lack of a subpulmonary ventricular pump, committing patients to a state of chronic circulatory
inefficiency progressively eventuating in heart, liver, and other organ failure. This proposal aims to bioengineer
a functional biological cardiac conduit with improved contractility and pressure generation by leveraging a novel
multi-axis nonplanar printing platform to recapitulate ventricular helical myocardial architecture. Completion of
this work will be an important step towards engineering regenerative cardiac tissues capable of effectively
replacing the missing ventricular pump in single-ventricle patients.

org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This K23 career development award will enhance Dr. David H. Lynch’s capacity to develop and implement

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE

application_id: 11162213
activity_code: F31
title: Early Identification of Neurodevelopmental Disorders with in utero MRI
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
In order to understand how the brain develops, both normally and abnormally, we need to gain insight into how the
brain changes in the period before and after birth. My proposal seeks to characterize neurodevelopment across this
important perinatal period, and provide information regarding the degree to which prenatal assessments of brain
structure relate to postnatal structure and function. This work has exciting implications for identifying fetal measures
useful for predicting neurodevelopmental disorders earlier than is usually possible.

application_id: 11009143
activity_code: F31
title: Exploring Acceptability & Potential Reach of Game-Based & Social Network Strategies for Improving PrEP & HIV Self-Testing Uptake among Latinx Sexual Minority Men Living in an EHE Priority Jurisdiction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research examines the configurations of online gaming and offline friendship networks of Latinx
men who have sex with men (LMSM) and the extent to which these network structures and compositions can
influence LMSM access to pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) information and promote the distribution of HIV self-
testing kits (HIVST). This mixed-methods study will utilize dyadic, egocentric, and two-mode network
visualizations and analyses and qualitative interview data to inform the development and implementation of
culturally-tailored game- and social network-based interventions to enhance HIV prevention services for LMSM
and other underserved communities. These findings will guide the development of the applicant’s future K99/R00
application.

title: Quantifying Psychological Resilience and Susceptibility to Predict Childhood Outcomes
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Social Determinants of Health (SDoH) are potent predictors of psychopathology; however,
what leaves individuals resilient and/or susceptible to SDoH-related psychopathology
remains poorly understood. The goal of my F31 is to develop a quantitative measure of
resilience/susceptibility to SDoH-related psychopathology and then characterize its
genomic and neural correlates. Ultimately, a better understanding of who is most vulnerable
to SDoH-related psychopathology and its biological correlates will further elucidate our
etiologic understanding of psychopathology to pave the way for future developments in
treatment, prevention, and policy.

application_id: 11068148
activity_code: F31
title: Structural Stigma and Mental Health Among Transgender and Gender Diverse Adults Living in the Rural United States
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Transgender and gender diverse (TGD) communities in the rural United States experience a disproportionate
burden of depression, anxiety, and suicidality due to stigma experienced on the structural level, including laws,
policies, social attitudes, and norms. Using both publicly available and survey data, this mixed methods study
will explore how recent state-level laws and policies focused on transgender healthcare, as well as the policy
environment more broadly, affect the mental health of TGD people. Findings from this study can inform future
policy decisions by demonstrating the impact of policies on mental health in the rural TGD community.

title: The network structure of childhood adversities and adult depressive symptoms in rural Uganda
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA

health research.

can better understand how and for whom depression is most likely to arise. Completion of the proposed research

application_id: 11163872
activity_code: F31
title: Differentiating Primary and Secondary Subtypes of Callous Unemotional Traits During Early Childhood
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Children with callous-unemotional (CU) traits can be differentiated into primary and secondary subtypes, which
are hypothesized to develop through distinct etiological pathways. This proposal examines how distinct patterns
of physiological reactivity, measured by parasympathetic nervous system function, and exposure to adversity
underpin these two subtypes of CU traits in early childhood. Findings can contribute to better knowledge of how
environmental and biological factors give rise to primary versus secondary CU traits, while also informing
precision diagnostics and targeted treatments within the field of childhood disruptive behavior disorders.

phr: Project Narrative
Negative symptoms, specifically experiential symptoms of reduced motivation and pleasure (MAP), are highly
prevalent in psychotic disorders and mood disorders with psychosis and are strongly linked to disability and
distress, yet treatments remain ineffective. This proposal seeks to employ four advanced methods— ecological
momentary assessment (EMA), repeatable mobile cognitive testing (RMCT), naturalistic fMRI stimuli, and
individual-specific functional connectivity (IS-FC)—to explore the connections between MAP symptoms with
neurocognition and functional connectivity in psychotic disorders and mood disorders with psychosis. This
project aims to further our understanding of the transdiagnostic cognitive and neurobiological bases of

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Treatment of the frequent indications for anticoagulation among adults with epilepsy is complicated by theoretical pharmacokinetics, as well as in vitro and animal data, that support interactions between widely-used enzyme- inducing antiepileptic drugs (EI-AEDs) and direct-acting oral anticoagulants (DOACs), which would be expected to lead to a reduction in the therapeutic effectiveness of DOACs and an elevated risk of thromboembolic events. title: Pancreatic fat and volume and childhood metabolic health
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Type 2 diabetes (T2D) and other metabolic diseases are becoming increasingly common among youth. Although obesity is a strong risk factor for metabolic dysfunction, only a fraction of youth with obesity develops T2D, suggesting factors beyond obesity are also at play. In this proposal, we will leverage an ongoing, longitudinal, pre-birth cohort study to examine variability in pancreas composition (fat fraction) and size (volume) in adolescence according to individual traits and prior nutrition/growth factors, and whether these pancreatic markers are predictive of dysglycemia onset during puberty.

application_id: 11317575
activity_code: F31
title: The Nerve of Chemo: Unpacking Chemotherapy-Induced Peripheral Neuropathy in Breast Cancer Survivors
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Breast cancer survivors are living longer due to advancements in cancer treatment, heightening the urgency
for more research on their ongoing health needs, particularly for Black breast cancer survivors who experience
significant differences in cancer treatment outcomes. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) is
a common side effect that significantly impacts daily life and quality of life, yet limited research exists on CIPN
among Black breast cancer survivors. This proposed study will examine CIPN's characteristics and severity in
Black breast cancer survivors, aiming to inform future longitudinal studies that guide intervention development
and improve treatment outcomes.

title: Testing the cellular mechanisms that underlie cerebellar synaptic plasticity and motor learning
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Decades of work in the cerebellar field have defined sensorimotor pathways required for motor learning,
characterized forms of plasticity at various synapses in the cerebellar circuit, identified molecular signaling
cascades involved in synaptic plasticity, and generated theoretical frameworks for how specific sites of synaptic
plasticity could enable motor learning. Despite these advances, technical limitations for measuring synaptic
responses in vivo have frustrated attempts to provide an overarching connection between synaptic plasticity
mechanisms within the cerebellar circuit and cerebellar motor learning. I will use a powerful combination of
cerebellar-dependent behavior, electrophysiology, and a novel biosensor technology to bridge molecular, circuit,
and behaviora...

title: Default Mode Network Inflammation as a Supraspinal Mechanism of Pain Chronification
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The underlying mechanisms of pain chronification after tissue or nerve injury remain elusive, especially those
mechanisms that involve supraspinal structures, limiting acute pain management to addressing the pain as a
symptom secondary to acute injury rather than a prelude to chronic illness. Using resting-state fMRI in awake
rats, we will examine Default Mode Network (DMN) connectivity changes that may contribute to the
progression of chronic neuropathic pain, complementing the clinical literature that has found abnormalities of
DMN connectivity in human patients with chronic pain. Localized DMN neuroinflammation will be examined as
a contributor to changes in DMN connectivity, extending previous research in the field that has characterized
supraspinal neuroinflammation ...

application_id: 11162601
activity_code: F31
title: Patient-Centered Neurological Evaluation of Functional MRI Outcomes in Multiple Sclerosis
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:
Multiple sclerosis (MS) is the most common central nervous system disease affecting young adults, with
significant gaps in spinal cord (SC) imaging necessitating advanced techniques like functional MRI (fMRI) to
better characterize MS pathology and clinical status. This study will apply resting-state SC fMRI as an
objective, quantifiable measure to assess motor and sensory function of the cervical SC in relapsing-remitting
MS, and how these neuronal circuits are associated with alterations in SC structural and vascular integrity.
Resting-state fMRI signatures arising in the MS SC are the only non-invasive evaluators of SC function, and
the relationship between resting state fMRI and neurological outcomes can provide a better understanding of
the human experience of MS.

application_id: 11164226
activity_code: F31
title: Evaluating the protein homeostasis of N-methyl-D-aspartate receptors containing pathogenic GluN2B subunits
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
N-methyl-D-aspartate receptors (NMDARs) act as switches in the brain, deciding when and how strongly
neurons communicate with one another, making them essential for learning, memory; when they malfunction,
neurological disorders such as autism spectrum disorder, depression, and Alzheimer’s can occur. We are
studying specific changes in these receptors to understand how they work, how cells build and discard these
proteins, and why these changes cause malfunctions. We aim to find ways to fix issues of inefficient assembly
by pinpointing affected pathways that can serve as specific targets for future drug therapies offering hope for
enhanced, targeted therapies, resulting in the better management of neurological conditions.

title: The role of astrocytes in prion strain susceptibility
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: We do not understand how the prion protein strains, although very similar in structure
act so differently in the brain. This project will help to understand prion strain susceptibility by examining

biology mechanisms of astrocytes and how this affects disease severity.

title: Investigating the Molecular and Cellular Basis of RNA Exosome-linked Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type1b in Human 3D Brain Organoids
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
All cells within the human body require the proper function of RNA-regulatory machines to ensure accurate gene
expression and cellular homeostasis. Mutations in ubiquitously expressed RNA-regulatory machinery
disproportionally cause severe brain disorders. Here, we utilize engineered human induced pluripotent stem cell-
derived 3D neuronal cultures modeling disease-causing mutations in the RNA-regulatory RNA exosome complex
to assess the functional consequences of these mutations on neuronal development and function.

application_id: 11162938
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanism of Altered Synaptic Signaling by Disease Related GRIN1 Variants
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project will characterize pathological variants in the GRIN1 gene from a molecular to synaptic level to
increase our understanding of how N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (NMDAR) activity contributes to health and
disease states. It will delineate the biophysical properties of receptors containing the GluN1 Y647C and Y647S
variants, as well as investigate pharmacological rescue with positive allosteric modulators in cultured cells and
brain slices. Results from this project will inform us about effects on receptor unitary conductance and gating
kinetics, whole cell current amplitude and time course, and effects on excitatory and inhibitory synaptic
transmission, which will further reveal the relationship between NMDAR signaling and brain function.

application_id: 11163951
activity_code: F31
title: The role of microglia in TRPV4 mediated neurodegeneration
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
I aim to characterize the identity and roles of microglia in TRPV4 (transient receptor potential vanilloid 4)-
mediated motor neuron degeneration. Neurodegeneration in mutant TRPV4 mice is caused by blood-CNS
barrier (BCNSB) breakdown and this mouse model provides an opportunity to characterize the specific microglial
subpopulations activated by vascular leak and their roles in disease. This work will advance our understanding
of neuroinflammatory processes triggered by BCNSB breakdown and potentially identify novel therapeutic
strategies.

org_name: MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY

title: Hunger-dependent modulation of neural circuits for social visuomotor processing in schooling fish
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The brain's ability to adjust sensitivity to sensory stimuli based on an animal’s internal state is crucial for
interactions with the environment. To explore how internal states influence complex natural sensorimotor
behaviors, this project will employ a multidisciplinary approach to investigate the hunger-dependent perception
of collective social motion in schooling fish. This research will offer insights on the intricate interplay between
sensory perception and appetite regulation in evolutionarily conserved neural circuits, potentially informing new
strategies to address obesity and malnutrition across diverse vertebrate species.

application_id: 11241571
activity_code: F31
title: Examining the role of synaptic facilitation in cortical network function and behavioral flexibility
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Neurological disorders often impair the brain’s ability to learn and adapt, which may be linked to changes in
how neuronal connections are rapidly reshaped on sub-second timescales. This project investigates how
disruptions in this process, called short-term plasticity, impact behavioral flexibility using both behavioral and
modeling approaches. The proposed experiments will improve our understanding of the role of short-term
plasticity in cognition, paving the way for novel diagnostic tools and treatments targeting this poorly understood
feature of neurotransmission.

application_id: 11240209
activity_code: F31
title: Characterizing clearance mechanisms of TDP-43 and disease associated isoforms
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ARIZONA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Proteinopathy of the RNA binding protein TDP-43 occurs in neuronal tissue in many neurodegenerative dis-
eases, including in >95% of all ALS cases, where it is understood to be a key driver of the disease. We recently
discovered a novel endolysosomal clearance mechanism that facilitates degradation of cytoplasmic TDP-43 via
ubiquitin-dependent targeting to multivesicular bodies. Here, I will uncover new mechanistic detail regarding
TDP-43 endolysosomal degradation and determine the clearance pathways of disease associated TDP-43
isoforms to reveal broader insight into cytoplasmic proteostasis and potentially identify therapeutic targets for
TDP-43 proteinopathies.

application_id: 11242859
activity_code: F31
title: Defining the regulation of exocytosis in developing neurons
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
During development brain cells grow rapidly and increase their surface area to establish the complex, elongated
shape required for brain function. When this expansion is disrupted, it can lead to improper synaptic connections,
contributing to neurodevelopmental disorders and psychiatric syndromes. This research proposal aims to
investigate how the surface area of developing brain cells increase to better understand the mechanisms driving
healthy brain development.

application_id: 11243650
activity_code: F31
title: Chromatin regulation of cell fate transitions during cortical development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
During development of the cerebral cortex – the portion of the brain responsible for conscious thought, action,
and perception – a myriad of diverse cells must acquire distinct molecular profiles, morphologies, connectivities,
and physiologic properties. These cellular identities are specified through a tightly organized, sequential process;
however, the precise mechanisms directing this process remain unclear. This project seeks to clarify the role of
one potential mechanism – the Polycomb Repressive Complex 2 – in the specification of cell identities during
cortical development, and to deliver a concise list of other promising candidates whose roles can be assessed
in future studies.

application_id: 10934354
activity_code: F31
title: Defining cell type-specific functions for the selective autophagy receptor p62 in neurons and astrocytes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative. Neurons and astrocytes have unique demands in regulating the quality of their proteome that require the autophagy degradation pathway, but how autophagy is regulated to facilitate cell type-specific functions and responses to stress is largely unknown. This proposal will elucidate cell type-specific functions for the autophagy receptor p62 in neurons and astrocytes, and how ALS-linked mutations in p62 impair p62 function in each cell type. Insights from this study will generate new ideas for how neurons and astrocytes use p62 differently in basal, stress, and disease conditions, and may uncover new and more precise cell-specific targets to modify autophagy for the treatment of neurodegenerative disease.

title: Biomaterial-directed patterning of xenografted brain organoids in-vivo
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Modeling the human brain with accuracy and reliability remains a key challenge in the field of neuroscience
and is the basis for our understanding of prevalent diseases such as Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, epilepsy, and
others. My dissertation project aims to improve a new type of model – xenotransplantation of human brain
organoids into rodent cortex – by directing cell patterning and neuronal circuit integration into host systems.
Results from this project will establish improved, human-centric models of brain development and contribute to
our collective understanding of the molecular mechanisms underlying human brain development.

title: Effects of Local Adaptation on Parallel Pathway Circuit Computations in the Retina
org_name: BARNARD COLLEGE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research aims to discover how biophysical adaptation mechanisms that permit the retina to
adapt its computational function contribute to neural encoding. These studies would help us to understand
how the circuits of the brain, more generally, flexibly navigate their computational objectives. In elucidating
part of the neural code that contributes to adaptable neural circuit function, this project will broadly benefit

title: Predicting the neurophysiological consequences of pathogenic SCN2A variants
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARATIVE
Mutations in the gene SCN2A, which gives rise to a protein in brain cells, have been implicated in a wide range
of brain diseases, including epilepsy and autism. In this project, we will (1) determine the effects of 115 SCN2A
mutations on protein function and brain cell activity using computational approaches, (2) validate the effect on
brain cell activity for a subset of these mutations using an experimental system, and (3) assess the feasibility of
using this system for testing the ability of drugs to correct abnormal brain cell activity. Our work will explain the
impact on and relationship between abnormal protein function and brain cell activity in diseases caused by
SCN2A mutations and will develop a drug testing system for evaluating therapies for each patient indivi...

application_id: 11166186
activity_code: F31
title: Elucidating the role of microglial Beta-hexosaminidase in microglial regulation of perineuronal nets in homeostasis and Alzheimer's disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative
 Certain types of neurons, many of which are important for memory, are surrounded by specialized
structures which protect them from toxic stressors and help stabilize their connections to other neurons. These
structures decrease in Alzheimer’s disease, which may leave the neurons more vulnerable and therefore more
likely to die. This project will improve our understanding of the means by which these structures are regulated
in the healthy brain and lost in disease.

activity_code: F31
title: Exploring the role of a premotor cell type for active sensor control in Drosophila
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The challenge, or advantage, of sensing during movement is as evolutionarily conserved as motile behavior
itself. The experiments proposed here will illuminate fundamental mechanisms of how the nervous system
integrates sensory information with motor plans for movement. By elucidating elementary circuit function in
Drosophila, this research will increase our understanding of how nervous systems use sensory information to
guide coordinated movement and behavior, which is crucial for understanding how these mechanisms fail in
disease.

neurodevelopmental disorder (NDD) called Arboleda-Tham Syndrome (ARTHS, OMIM#616268) when mutated

application_id: 11111269
activity_code: T32
title: Training Program in Cell and Molecular Biology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Cell and Molecular Biology Training Grant seeks to broadly train PhD students in cell and molecular
biology to adapt to changing needs in the biomedical research work force. This Predoctoral Training Program
includes education and research opportunities to investigate the molecular and cellular basis of fundamental
biological processes that range from cell signaling and architecture to gene expression, employing a wide array
of model systems and state of the art imaging, molecular/cellular assays, and high throughput technologies.
Career development opportunities are also central to the training program.

title: The Impact of Natural Sequence Variation in HSV-2 Gamma 34.5 on Neurovirulence
org_name: LURIE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Herpes simplex virus type 2 (HSV-2) infections in the neonatal brain often result in encephalitis and permanent
neurodevelopmental deficits, yet the factors driving these infections remain poorly understood. The goal of the
proposed study is to determine how natural sequence variations in the HSV-2 34.5 gene impact the functions
of the ICP34.5 and  proteins, as well as the neurovirulence of the virus itself. The results of this study will help
guide the development of novel therapies which are critically needed to protect neonates from the devastating
consequences of HSV-2 neurological infections.

application_id: 11161001
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanisms of Noninvasive Ultrasonic Neuromodulation of Parkinsonian Neural Circuits
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Recent progress in transcranial ultrasound (TUS) has brought increasing excitement to the potential of
developing a new generation of non-invasive neuromodulation for Parkinson's disease (PD). The goal of this
research proposal is to understand how TUS modulates neuronal dynamics using in-vivo cellular imaging with
single-cell, single-spike level precision in the motor cortex and striatum of awake, behaving mice, as well as
characterizing its impact on dopaminergic transmission and motor function. This work could provide invaluable
insight into how PD neural circuits respond to TUS in both pathological and nonpathological states, offering a
mechanistic understanding for therapeutic intervention.

application_id: 11162627
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating Molecular Mechanisms of Alternating Hemiplegia of Childhood Using C. elegans Models
org_name: BROWN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mutations in ATP1A3, a Na+, K+ ATPase, cause Alternating Hemiplegia of Childhood (AHC) through an
unknown mechanism. In this proposal, I use C. elegans as a model organism to investigate properties of the
Na+, K+ ATPase and determine how AHC patient mutations impair normal function. Completion of this project
will provide foundational information for future studies that delineate the cellular mechanisms perturbed in AHC
and investigate targets for therapy development.

application_id: 11165061
activity_code: F31
title: Defining the degradative and inflammatory responses to mitochondrial damage in astrocytes.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
Astrocytes are non-neuronal cells in the brain that heavily influence neuronal health, and that have
been increasingly appreciated to play a role in neurodegenerative diseases like Parkinson’s disease.
One key aspect of Parkinson’s disease is dysfunction of mitochondria in neurons and astrocytes;
although this has been extensively studied in neurons, we do not understand how astrocytes clear
damaged mitochondria or whether astrocytes that have sustained this type of damage release
inflammatory molecules toxic to neurons. This project will address these questions, thus providing
information about how astrocytes respond to mitochondrial damage that will help us understand
astrocytic contributions to Parkinson’s disease pathology.

activity_code: T32
title: Training Program in Cell and Molecular Biology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The Cell and Molecular Biology Training Grant seeks to broadly train PhD students in cell and molecular biology to adapt to changing needs in the biomedical research work force. This Predoctoral Training Program includes education and research opportunities to investigate the molecular and cellular basis of fundamental biological processes that range from cell signaling and architecture to gene expression, employing a wide array of model systems and state of the art imaging, molecular/cellular assays, and high throughput technologies. Career development opportunities are also central to the training program.

application_id: 10863603
activity_code: T32
title: Research Training in Physiological Adaptations to Stress
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The role of stress as the underlying cause for human adaptation is well-appreciated. Inherent in this response is the logical progression to human disease and disability, as there is little debate regarding the link between many different types of stress and the natural history of disease initiation and progression. The proposed program will focus on predoctoral scientist training in common themes of stress adaptation, and emphasize new core competencies that will develop new research skill sets, preparing scientists to innovate and discover novel therapeutic interventions.
terms: Physiological Adaptation;Research Training;Stress

application_id: 11164127
activity_code: F31
title: Early life influenza infection and glial dysregulation
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Each year, millions of children in the United States contract influenza, and while these viruses primarily infect
the respiratory tract, they can also affect the central nervous system. Early postnatal life is a critical period during
which the brain undergoes significant development, and immune challenges, such as influenza, can potentially
disrupt this process with long-lasting effects. This proposed study aims to identify the long-term neurobiological
impacts of early postnatal influenza infection on synapse development, myelin plasticity, and glial dysregulation.
