application_id: 11165909
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the RNA-Regulatory Activities of the RNA Exosome during Human Motor Neuron Differentiation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative:
The RNA exosome is a ubiquitously expressed RNA-regulatory complex essential for cellular differentiation in
humans. Inherited mutations in RNA exosome subunit genes cause devastating neurodevelopmental diseases,
several of which cause severe motor neuron defects. To investigate the role of the RNA exosome in
neurodevelopment, we will use human motor neurons modeling disease-causing RNA exosome mutations in
vitro to assess the role of the complex during motor neuron development and provide insight into RNA exosome-
linked motor neuropathy.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The goal of the Texas Doctoral Bridge Program, a formal partnership between Hispanic Serving Institutions Texas State University (master's degree granting institution) and the University of Texas Health Science Center at San Antonio (UTHSA) (Ph.D. granting institution), is to increase the number of students from historically marginalized groups (HMGs) who complete doctoral studies in the biomedical sciences. application_id: 11317346
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating Rab8 Phosphorylation and AMPA Receptor Trafficking in the LRRK2G2019S Mutation
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Elevations in LRRK2 kinase activity are causally related to Parkinson's, but the cellular mechanisms that are
targeted to produce disease symptoms are poorly understood. The proposed work will generate neuronal
maps of LRRK2 activity and pathology and will identify sites and targets of dysfunctional cellular trafficking
mechanisms that are unable to support normal synapse plasticity. These data will help to identify molecular
pathways that may be recruited or suppressed to preserve cognitive function or ameliorate symptoms in
Parkinson's.

application_id: 10828444
activity_code: K08
title: Physiology of Lifespan Extension and Metabolic Hormesis with Riboflavin Depletion
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Calorie restriction has been shown to be beneficial to healthspan and lifespan in multiple animal models. We have shown that depletion of riboflavin, also known as Vitamin B2, extends lifespan in the nematode worm in a model similar to calorie restriction. This project serves to investigate the mechanisms of how this occurs and whether riboflavin depletion can promote similar benefits in mammals, with the hope of identifying new treatments for aging associated diseases.

The role of stress as the underlying cause for human adaptation is well-appreciated. Inherent in
this response is the logical progression to human disease and disability, as there is little debate
regarding the link between many different types of stress and the natural history of disease initiation
and progression. The proposed program will focus on predoctoral scientist training in common
themes of stress adaptation, and emphasize new core competencies that will develop new research
skill sets, preparing scientists to innovate and discover novel therapeutic interventions.
terms: <Physiological Adaptation><Research Training><Stress>
---
application_id: 11115595
activity_code: T32
title: Genetics and Genomics of Human Disease
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Rapid and inexpensive DNA sequencing technologies have revolutionized medicine by revealing the
landscape of mutations that contribute to human disease. Over the next decade, whole genome sequencing of
many thousands of individuals, coupled with novel genome editing approaches to determine the impact of
specific human mutations in experimental model systems, will provide unprecedented insight into disease
mechanisms and empower new strategies for prevention, diagnosis and treatment. This training program will
train the next generation of scientists to be able to take full advantage of this sea change in human genetics
and prepare them for productive careers in science that will lead to improved human health.
terms: <Genetic><Genomics><human disease>
---
application_id: 11318025
activity_code: F31
title: Determine the Role of Microglia in Adult Circuit Remodeling in a Mouse Model of Whisker Denervation
org_name: HENRY M. JACKSON FDN FOR THE ADV MIL/MED
phr: Project Narrative
Loss of peripheral sensory input leads to significant alterations in neuronal activity and widespread circuit
reorganization. Microglia actively shape circuits by communicating with neurons and other glia to remove
underused synapses. Our findings will identify how microglia shape denervation-induced circuit reorganization,
help us better understand healthy adult plasticity, and will identify potential treatments that support recovery
after injuries that cause the loss of peripheral sensation.

application_id: 11165943
activity_code: F31
title: Neural Circuits for Evaluating Complex Motor Sequences in Female Songbirds
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
During courtship, female songbirds evaluate the quality of male suitors by rapidly forming an internal
representation of the suitor song and comparing acoustic features across renditions. Understanding the neural
computations that enable this complex task will address a fundamental, unresolved question in neuroscience:
how do our brains evaluate the behavior of another? This project will unite experimental and computational
approaches to establish the female songbird as a novel model for the neural basis of social evaluation and
provide central insights into how the brain processes and acts on social cues, and how this may fail in social
disorders like autism spectrum disorder.

title: Interrogating the necessity of neuronal contributions to SCA3
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
My research project seeks to define the necessity of neuronal expression of mutant ATXN3 in spinocerebellar
ataxia type 3 (SCA3) using a novel conditional knock-in mouse model. By selectively silencing mutant ATXN3
in neurons and assessing the resulting motor deficits and pathological changes, I aim to elucidate the cellular
mechanisms driving SCA3. This project will not only offer critical insights for therapeutic development but also
equip me with valuable skills in experimental design, data analysis, and scientific communication essential for
a career in neurodegeneration research.

application_id: 11240827
activity_code: F31
title: Deciphering SPTAN1-associated disease mechanisms and potential therapy in patient-derived cell lines
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
The project serves to determine how the c.6908_6916dup (p.D2303_L2305dup) and the c.6619_6621del
(p.E2207del) mutations in SPTAN1, a subunit that is a part of the large spectrin protein helping to stabilize and
organize neuronal membranes, cause severe developmental epileptic encephalopathy type 5 (DEE5).
Specifically, this project will investigate the molecular interactors of SPTAN1 and determine the specific and
effective therapeutic targets within individuals to alleviate the phenotypes associated with the mutations. The
goal of this project is to reduce the burden of disease in individuals affected by the mutations described above
by providing preclinical evidence of disease mechanisms and therapeutic strategies for potential clinical
development.

application_id: 11242684
activity_code: F31
title: Elucidating the Role of Mechanosensitive Ion Channels in Ultrasound Stimulation Treatments for Neuroinflammatory Injury and Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This training plan aims to assess non-invasive low-intensity pulsed ultrasound stimulation (LIPUS) as a potential
therapeutic intervention to reduce chronic neuroinflammation, a critical factor affecting tissue health in brain injury
and disease. Nearly 1 in 5 Americans have a neurological condition, which poses a significant burden on national
public health systems. Advancing our understanding of LIPUS and elucidating its mechanisms will assist in
establishing it as a safe and effective treatment, mitigating the impact of neuroinflammatory diseases on our
population, and thereby reducing healthcare costs and improving quality of life.

application_id: 11244903
activity_code: F31
title: Cortical Integration in Humans: An Intracranial Investigation of Neural Synchrony
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Disruptions in cortical integration are associated with a variety of neurological and psychiatric disorders that
are poorly understood and urgently need better treatment options, such as schizophrenia, dementia, ADHD,
and many more. Furthermore, due to ethical reasons, much of the research that is performed to study the basic
neurophysiology underlying these diseases is performed in rodents. Developing a better understanding of the
human neurophysiology underlying the processes which are disrupted is essential to developing efficacious
treatments for them and the research highlighted in this proposal has the potential to inform such models and
treatments.

application_id: 10789851
activity_code: F32
title: Regulating cell-cell interactions to improve donor retinal ganglion cell integration
org_name: SCHEPENS EYE RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: Project Narrative The mammalian retina does not possess the capacity to regenerate; thus, progressive retinal neuron death leads to irreversible vision loss in glaucoma optic neuropathies and other degenerative diseases of the retina and optic nerve. Cell transplantation has been proposed to restore the retinal neurocircuitry; however, current state-of-the- art studies show that most donor retinal ganglion cells (RGCs) do not integrate into the ganglion cell layer and connect with the existing host retinal neurons. This research proposal will investigate the role that Down Syndrome Cell Adhesion Molecule (DSCAM) has in directing donor RGC migration and synapse formation with their natural connecting points in the retina.

application_id: 11195013
activity_code: DP5
title: Response and resistance to chimeric antigen receptor T cell therapy in human solid tumors using spatial multi-omics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project narrative
 Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy has revolutionized the treatment of blood cancer, but it has
yet to demonstrate efficacy in solid tumors, and our understanding of barriers is limited. I have developed an
integrated spatial multi-omic analytical pipeline that I will apply to a comprehensive biobank from five CAR T-
cell clinical trials to define the tumor microenvironment with greater precision than previously possible. My
preliminary data suggest that CAR T-cell therapy fundamentally reorganizes the solid tumor microenvironment,
and my proposed work will reveal mechanisms of resistance and response to guide the development of next-
generation CAR T-cell therapies.

title: Trehalose-based coacervates for biomolecular and cellular delivery to improve wound healing in spinal cord injury
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Spinal cord injury (SCI) leads to irreversible damage of neural tissue and the formation of a non-neural lesion
core that prevents neural regeneration through the lesion site. I have developed trehalose coacervates, a unique
polymer-based molecular and cellular delivery vehicle that can deliver high concentrations of bioactive cargo to
a local tissue bed without causing disruption to healthy neural tissue. In this project, I will optimize trehalose
coacervates for biomolecular and cellular delivery to augment the SCI would response by directing regeneration-
supportive glia repair at the lesion core in two ways: (1) coacervate-based molecular co-delivery to enhance
proliferation of endogenous injury-responsive astrocytes after SCI, and (2) coacervate-based enzymatic
debr...

application_id: 11313023
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating Neuroprotective Roles of BCAT1 During Hypoxia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Preliminary work from our group has revealed that neuronal BCAA metabolism is disrupted by prenatal hypoxia.
This project aims to characterize hypoxia’s effects on BCAA metabolism, epigenetic signatures, and oxidative
stress, focusing on the role of branched chain amino acid transaminase 1 (BCAT1). Elucidating the function of
BCAT1 in the neuronal hypoxic response will provide insights into the role of BCAA metabolism in the cellular
hypoxic response and will provide crucial insight into the therapeutic potential for supplementation or restriction
of BCAA content in infant formula for prenatal hypoxia.

application_id: 11311535
activity_code: F31
title: Cellular and circuit effects of genetic ablation of adult-born oligodendrocytes
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
During this fellowship, I am exploring the biology of adult-born oligodendrocytes (OLs), a large population of non-
neuronal cells in the central nervous system responsible for insulating axons in myelin and shuttling critical
resources to the underlying neuron. I am using a combination of in vivo imaging and behavioral paradigms,
leveraging a novel mouse line I recently generated in the lab, to provide critical insight into the generation and
loss dynamics of adult-born OLs and their potential roles in homeostatic regulation of memory circuitry. This work
will further our understanding of OL death dynamics and the functional implications of such loss, generating
foundational knowledge with potential implications for understanding and treating demyelinating diseases.

application_id: 11115838
activity_code: T32
title: Interdisciplinary Training in Demography
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed program will train new cohorts of demographers to study contemporary issues in
population health and wellbeing. Demography is the study of population size, structure, and
change. Most contemporary social problems have demographic components, as for example
differential fertility rates, rising mortality rates, or changes in migration. By understanding
dynamics of mortality, fertility, migration, marriage and divorce, we are better able to develop
social policy that promotes child health and family wellbeing.

application_id: 10763421
activity_code: K08
title: NFkB dynamics in the stimulus specificity of trained immunity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cells of the innate immune system can be reprogrammed by exposure to stimuli such as cytokines or pathogens in a phenomenon known as “trained immunity.” This can make immune cells either more inflammatory or less inflammatory in response to subsequent infections, but what determines whether a given stimulus will result in potentiated or diminished inflammation is unclear. This proposal explores the hypothesis that dynamic features of the transcription factor NFκB – not if it is activated, but how it is activated – contribute to the stimulus-specificity of trained immunity through the regulation of latent enhancers that control gene expression.

to cardiac dysfunction, that occur in a single RNA exosome gene to understand and compare how these changes

Chronic alcohol (ethanol) exposure results in changes in brain structure and function that contributes to long-
term excessive drinking behaviors. I hypothesize that chronic ethanol exposure increases the neural activity of
orexinergic neurons projecting from the lateral hypothalamic area to the ventral tegmental area that leads to
ethanol misuse. Utilizing cutting-edge scientific approaches, I will identify and characterize changes in the
orexinergic neurons that innervate the ventral tegmental area and their role in excessive ethanol drinking.

application_id: 11242849
activity_code: F32
title: Advanced Imaging and Assistive Devices to Uncover and Address Novel Mechanisms of Age-Related Mobility Deficits
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The premise of this proposal is that widespread and poorly understood age-related gait deficits arise in part due
to impaired foot function from substantial toe flexor weakness in the older adult population. The proposed
approach combines state-of-the-art biomedical measurement modalities, clinical gait analysis, low-cost assistive
devices, and high fidelity computational musculoskeletal simulation to best parse the underlying mechanisms of
mobility impairment. The expected results will have an immediate impact on clinical targets for diminished gait
capacity and our understanding of age-related deficits in foot function.

title: Interdisciplinary Training in Demography
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed program will train new cohorts of demographers to study contemporary issues in population health and wellbeing. Demography is the study of population size, structure, and change. Most contemporary social problems have demographic components, as for example differential fertility rates, rising mortality rates, or changes in migration. By understanding dynamics of mortality, fertility, migration, marriage and divorce, we are better able to develop social policy that promotes child health and family wellbeing.

application_id: 11164284
activity_code: F32
title: Reducing Misdiagnosis in Alzheimer's Disease: AI-Driven Biomarker Identification
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The goal is to reduce the occurrence of misdiagnosis, revolutionize diagnostic accuracy, enable early
interventions, and enhance the efficiency of healthcare systems in managing Alzheimer's disease (AD). The
core hypothesis of this project is that by leveraging the SILVER-seq technology to process limited-volume
scarce samples (often left over from previous studies that are not enough to be used by traditional techniques
anymore) and generate extracellular RNA (exRNA) data, it is possible to address critical gaps in AD research.
This approach aims to construct a multi-dimensional database, identify crucial exRNA markers for precision
diagnosis, and validate robust and reliable biomarkers for future practical applications.

title: Traumatic Brain Injury, Social Relationships, and Risk of Dementia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Traumatic brain injury (TBI) affects everyone in an individual’s social network and represents a significant,
modifiable risk factor for Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRDs) along with social isolation, but the
impact of interpersonal factors (e.g., social relationships) on associations of TBI with dementia risk is unclear.
The overarching goal of this proposal is to examine the role of social relationships with respect to dementia risk
and ADRD-related biomarker trajectories both in the context of an aging individual with TBI and among spouses
(as a surrogate for caregivers) of individuals who sustain a TBI. The overall hypotheses are that greater mid-life
social engagement will attenuate associations of TBI with risk of later-life dementia, and that spouses...

application_id: 11245061
activity_code: F32
title: Transmission dynamics and fitness of reemerging St. Louis encephalitis virus in mosquito vectors and West Nile virus native and immune avian reservoirs
org_name: COLORADO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The avian-mosquito-avian cycling flavivirus St. Louis encephalitis virus (SLEV) that infects humans to cause
febrile illness and fatal encephalitis has reemerged and persisted in the US starting in 2015. To understand
drivers of reemergence, this project will define infection and transmission dynamics of contemporary SLEV
strains in mosquito and avian hosts and the protective role of antibody derived from prior SLEV or related West
Nile virus infections in avian hosts. Understanding SLEV transmission dynamics can be applied to predict
future emergence events, direct vector control efforts to mitigate spread, and enhance antibody-centric
therapeutics to reduce human morbidity and mortality.

application_id: 11162184
activity_code: F32
title: SMAC Mimetics as Broad-Spectrum Antivirals
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Antivirals that are currently in clinical use target a limited number of viruses, have moderate efficacy and are
prone to resistance, therefore the discovery of potent, broad-spectrum antivirals with a high barrier to resistance
remains an urgent, unmet medical need for the treatment of endemic and emerging viral infections. This project
focuses on using SMAC mimetics as broad-spectrum antivirals for RNA viruses of pandemic potential. The
proposed studies will contribute to identifying the mechanisms and pathways utilized by SMAC mimetics and
enable their further optimization as broad-acting antivirals.

application_id: 11165811
activity_code: F32
title: Mechanisms of sigma-factor-dependent transcriptional control in Mycobacterium tuberculosis
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Sigma-factors are critical protein components of the bacterial transcription machinery, reprograming the
transcriptome to adapt to different environmental cues and stresses encountered from the host. The primary
sigma-factor in Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb), the causative bacterial agent of tuberculosis (TB), contains
an intrinsically disordered region (IDR) of unknown function. The proposed work will evaluate the structural
dynamics and function of this IDR, in effort to understand its role in Mtb transcriptional regulation and bacterial
resistance against the current primary antibiotic used to treat TB infections.

application_id: 11085723
activity_code: F32
title: Epigenetic Regulation of KSHV
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Kaposi’s sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV) causes 3 human cancers including Kaposi’s sarcoma, primary
effusion lymphoma, and multicentric Castleman’s disease. Tight viral gene epigenetic regulation is vital for
disease development and represents an underexplored area in KSHV basic virology. We propose to investigate
KSHV DNA methylation dynamics to inform our understanding of KSHV virology and with an aim to develop a
novel biomarker for KSHV associated diseases.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARATIVE
Tumor necrosis factor (TNF) superfamily members are key regulators of B cell biology, and blockade of several
of these pathways has shown significant benefit in autoimmune disease with minimal infectious risk. The TNF
superfamily member RANK and its ligand RANKL are highly expressed by B cells, impact B cell responses in
mice, and genetic variants in RANK associate with autoimmune disease; yet, RANK-RANKL control of human
B cell biology is largely unexplored. By harnessing samples from patients receiving an FDA-approved
osteoporosis medication that blocks RANKL, leveraging standard of care vaccination as a lens through which
to study B cell responses, and applying cutting-edge techniques to track changes in B cell specificity and
function following RANKL-blockade, this wor...

application_id: 11165449
activity_code: F32
title: Revealing the Structural Determinants of TCR Cross-Recognition via Extended Positional Scanning
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NOTRE DAME
phr: Project Narrative
Recognition of unexpected antigenic targets by T cell receptors (TCR) can lead to dangerous outcomes for
patients during the development of TCR-based therapeutics. Our understanding of the structural and
chemical basis for this cross-reactivity exhibited by TCRs represents a major gap that this proposal intends
to address. Over three aims, this work will develop an extended positional scanning library method to drive
the development and validation of new predictive models trained on datasets with unprecedented
physicochemical diversity, allowing us to more completely capture the range of targets a TCR can and
cannot recognize.

title: Training Program in Developmental and Stem Cell Biology
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This is a proposal for the competitive renewal of a T32 supported Training Program in Developmental
and Stem Cell Biology at the Weill Cornell Graduate School of Medical Sciences. The existing, inter-
disciplinary research programs in Developmental Biology, Stem Cell Biology and Systems Biology at
the Graduate School are outstanding, highly interactive, and have outstanding faculty and pre- and
post-doctoral trainees. To support the next generation of academic leaders in biomedical and
translational sciences, it is imperative to provide the highest level of organized instruction through a
T32-supported training program in developmental and stem cell biology, tissue differentiation,
organogenesis, systems biology, and regenerative medicine.

application_id: 11242333
activity_code: F32
title: Characterizing the Renal Immune Microenvironment in Antibody-Mediated Rejection to Predict Treatment Response using Imaging Mass Cytometry
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Kidney transplant rejection involves complex local immune mechanisms that can ultimately result in the loss of
the transplanted organ, affecting up to 30% of transplanted kidneys in the US. This proposal aims to profile the
renal immune landscape in biopsy samples from both stable grafts and those showing signs of rejection with the
goal to identify the specific immune responses that contribute to antibody-mediated rejection (ABMR) and
develop predictive tools for treatment response. This crucial information could lead to targets for new diagnostics
and therapeutics, ultimately improving patient outcomes and reducing the burden of graft loss on the healthcare
system.

title: Control of CD8+ T cell Memory Homeostasis via Extracellular Damage Sensors ARTC2 & P2RX7
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE
A significant proportion of the hematopoietic compartment is found not within the circulatory or lymphoid
systems, but as resident cells in non-lymphoid tissues where they act as first responders against invading
pathogens and to fulfill tissue-specific homeostatic roles in response to local inflammatory cues. Some of these
evolutionarily conserved ‘danger signals’ are released by tissue injury and are suspected to modify the survival
of resident T cells that represent a facet of the immunologic ‘memory’ that protects us from re-exposures such
as previous infections and cancer recurrence. This proposal will address the ill-defined role of one such
‘danger signal’ sensor, ARTC2.2, and how it exerts control over tissue-resident CD8+ T cell development,
maintenance, an...

application_id: 11243773
activity_code: F32
title: Characterization of PR3 Epitopes for the Precision Targeting of Autoreactive B cells in ANCA-Associated Vasculitis
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Advancing more accurate diagnostic and prognostic tests for antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA)-asso-
ciated vasculitis (AAV) remains challenging due to a limited knowledge of the heterogeneous pathogenic poten-
tial of autoantibodies targeting proteinase 3 (PR3), a serine protease expressed on the surface of neutrophils.
Moreover, all current treatment strategies are broadly immunosuppressive and leave patients vulnerable to se-
vere infection, a major cause of mortality in AAV. This proposed work aims to characterize the features of path-
ogenic autoantibodies and to develop a precision cellular immunotherapy, CATCR-T cells, for the selective elim-
ination of pathogenic anti-PR3 B cells in AAV.

title: Career Training in Reproductive Biology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
This training grant will increase the number of scientists trained in reproductive biology, and prepared for a
variety of career positions. This is important as it prepares scientists to participate in basic research,

application_id: 11163853
activity_code: F31
title: Parsing molecular determinants of asymmetry in planar cell polarity signaling
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Congenital heart diseases make up nearly half of all birth defects, and present a significant economic burden,
amongst which conotruncal heart malformations are the most common. This proposal investigates molecular
mechanisms behind planar cell polarity signaling, which is necessary for proper heart development and
implicated in the pathology of conotruncal heart malformations. Understanding the precise functions of these
proteins opens up opportunities for therapeutic development in addition to contributing to knowledge of this key

activity_code: K99
title: Emergent Behavior in a Dish: Discovery of Bi-directional Spiraling as a Population Phenomenon in C. elegans Enables In-Depth Dissection of Mechanisms Underlying Group Behaviors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Many individuals following a set of rules about how to interact with one another and the environment can give
rise to a group entity with new properties in a phenomenon known as emergence. To produce emergent
behavior, animals must communicate with multiple other individuals while simultaneously processing sensory
cues from the environment and then integrate this information into a cohesive behavioral output. While
emergent behaviors have traditionally been challenging to study, my discovery of bidirectional spiraling as a
novel emergent behavior in the highly tractable nematode C. elegans presents an opportunity to elucidate the
communication mechanisms and neural dynamics underlying a collective behavior with implications for
disorders marked by failure to integrate sen...

application_id: 11244497
activity_code: F32
title: Capsid assembly mechanisms of Kaposi Sarcoma associated herpesvirus
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Capsid assembly is an essential step in the herpesvirus replication cycle; however, little is understood on how
herpesvirus capsids nucleate and mature to form infectious C capsids. The proposed investigation will develop
an in-vitro platform to study the gammaherpesivus, Kaposi Sarcoma Herpesvirus Virus (KSHV), capsid kinetics
and assembly requirements to determine the similarities and differences between herpesvirus subfamilies.
KSHV reactivation is detrimental to the immunocompromised and there are no specific treatment options
available; this study will develop a scalable assay to testing herpesvirus capsid inhibitors to prevent lytic
replication and limit viral induced disease.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Based on pre-clinical and clinical reports of circadian disruptions in cancer hosts in combination with our
preliminary findings implicating muscle clocks to cachectic muscle, this project aims to investigate the role of
the muscle circadian clock in Cancer Cachexia (CC) progression, a condition affecting muscle mass and
function in up to 80% of cancer patients. Utilizing circadian reporter mice and inducible muscle-specific genetic
models, the study will define stage and muscle-specific changes in the muscle clock during CC development
and assess its contribution to disease onset and progression. By integrating expertise in circadian biology and
CC research, this training opportunity not only advances the scientific understanding of cancer-induced muscle

application_id: 11145718
activity_code: K23
title: Inhibitory Control and Externalizing Behaviors in Youth at Risk for Huntington Disease
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Recent advances toward potential disease-modifying therapies in Huntington disease (HD)
present an urgent need to identify early, quantifiable markers of disease progression in youth
and young adults at risk for HD. The goals of this study are to characterize impulsive behaviors
as an early marker of HD and to examine the influence of genetic status, environmental
stressors, and inhibitory control as potential predictors of these behaviors. These results will
lead to better understanding and improved detection of the earliest symptoms of HD, which will
help to guide the development of new therapies for those at risk of developing this fatal disease.

application_id: 11071199
activity_code: F32
title: Investigating the Role of Mutagenic DNA Repair during the Persistence of Cancer Cells
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Persistence is a transcriptionally distinct cellular state that occurs in response to environmental stressors and
toxins, similar to the SOS response in bacteria. Exhibiting slowed cell proliferation, upregulation of low-fidelity
DNA polymerases, and hypermutability of the genome, small populations of persister cancer cells survive
prolonged toxic onslaughts from anti-cancer drugs and expand once the stress is removed or after acquiring a
resistance-conferring mutation. Here, I aim to undertake a systematic evaluation of mutagenic DNA repair
pathways that contribute to persistence in cancer cells, thereby identifying potential new targets for anti-cancer
therapy to prevent acquired drug resistance and establishing a morphological signature of persistence.

application_id: 11083605
activity_code: F30
title: Investigating Cellular Senescence at the Single Cell Level
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT SCH OF MED/DNT
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Senescent cells are growth arrested cells that are highly associated with aging and chronic diseases and could
be targeted to slow the aging process. The transcriptomic signatures of senescent cells are not well defined
which has implications for designing treatments to treat aging related diseases. This proposal will leverage
single nucleus RNA-sequencing techniques in order to understand the molecular markers that define
senescent cells and may lead to the development of more efficacious therapies to target senescent cells.

application_id: 11163800
activity_code: F32
title: Relative and synergistic contributions of PD1 and LAG3 on CD4+ T conventional cells in cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Targeting the inhibitory receptors programmed cell-death-1 (PD1) and lymphocyte-activation gene-3 (LAG3)
expressed on the surface of exhausted (dysfunctional) T cells have represented a significant advancement in
cancer immunotherapy for melanoma, and this approach is being tested in a growing number of cancer types.
However, it is unclear how targeting PD1 and LAG3 together leads to improved anti-tumor responses, and why
not all patients respond. This proposal aims to determine how PD1 and LAG3 impact the function of CD4+ T
cells, an immune cell important in anti-tumor responses, to advance our understanding of how blockade of these
receptors might reinvigorate CD4+ T cell function with the hope to identify novel therapeutic targets to further
improve outcomes.

title: Extending Capacity for Affordable, Accessible Hearing Care through Peer Mentorship
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Significant hearing health care disparities exist among older adults by race/ethnicity and socioeconomic
position but community health worker models of care, as an efficacious approach to addressing disparities,
have been underutilized within hearing care. This innovative and multidisciplinary proposal will advance the
development and testing of a theory-driven, evidence-based hearing care intervention delivered by older adult
peer mentors that leverages the growing market of over-the-counter hearing technology. The proposal will
position the hearing care intervention for future implementation and scaling within a national network of aging
service providers.

application_id: 11163958
activity_code: F32
title: Characterizing Cancer Associated Fibroblast Evolution, Programming, and Fibroblast-Epithelial Cell Crosstalk in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pancreatic cancer is a deadly human malignancy with an overall 5-year survival rate of just 12.8%. Pancreatic
tumors are characterized by a dense desmoplastic stroma with a prevalence of Cancer Associated Fibroblasts
(CAFs). Here, we will investigate and map fibroblasts from normal pancreatic tissue to pancreatic ductal
adenocarcinoma and elucidate whether pancreatic tumors directly program these cells; in doing so, we hope to
contribute to critical gaps in our understanding of this disease.

application_id: 11162894
activity_code: F32
title: Evolution and clinical significance of complex genome rearrangements
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Genomic instability, a hallmark of cancer, drives profound genomic rearrangement and structural variation during
tumor evolution. Unlike point mutations and large, low-level copy number alterations, structural variants (and the
focal amplifications they often produce) are relatively understudied in the context of tumor evolutionary recon-
struction. Elucidating the temporal trends in the evolution of tumors' genomic structure will reveal drivers of
cancer progression, provide critical new therapeutic targets and predictive and prognostic biomarkers, and ulti-
mately improve outcomes for cancer patients.

application_id: 11161044
activity_code: F32
title: Defining the direct impact of m6A deposition on messenger RNA
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
The RNA modification N6-methyladenosine (m6A) has been reported to impact virtually every part of the mRNA
life cycle and is misregulated across a variety of cancer types. However, these pleiotropic effects have made it
challenging to disentangle the direct versus indirect functions of m6A. In this proposal we will use fast-acting
inhibitors of the METTL3 methyltransferase to investigate how m6A directly affects RNA biogenesis in multiple
AML cellular models, to comprehensively characterize the role of m6A in the expression of cancer driver genes.

application_id: 11241752
activity_code: F32
title: Elucidating the role of PATZ1 in MYCN-amplified neuroblastoma
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative
Neuroblastoma is the most common extra-cranial solid tumor affecting children, and the overall 5-year survival
for patients with high-risk neuroblastoma, which is characterized by MYCN-amplification, remains at about 50%.
Current therapies are aggressive with lifelong consequences, prompting the urgent need for improved therapies
for treating neuroblastoma. This project aims to validate PATZ1 as a dependency in neuroblastoma, uncover the
role of PATZ1 in disease pathogenicity, and biochemically characterize the epigenetic and transcriptional
consequences of PATZ1 loss in neuroblastoma, which could lead to a new therapeutic approach for treating
neuroblastoma.

application_id: 11245278
activity_code: F32
title: Early-Life Enterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis Acquisition and Colorectal Cancer Risk
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
B. fragilis is a commensal of the human colonic microbiome that is acquired in the first few weeks of life.
Enterotoxigenic variants of B. fragilis (ETBF) cause colonic crypt remodeling and have been associated with
colorectal cancer (CRC) progression. This proposal will focus on the link between early-life ETBF colonization
and CRC development later in life, identifying novel approaches to prevent CRC using mechanistic knowledge
of the ETBF-CRC association.

title: A postdoctoral training program for impactful careers in stem cell biology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA SANTA CRUZ
phr: Narrative This grant will support 5 postdocs (4 NIH-sponsored and 1 UCSC-sponsored) at UC Santa Cruz’ Institute for the Biology of Stem Cells to undergo rigorous research training and targeted career skill development in order to succeed in careers spanning from academic research to biotechnology and advocacy. An important aspect of our program is structured mentoring which has been shown to improve career outcomes and increase the motivation of postdoctoral fellows to enter the academic job market, especially for women and minority postdoctoral fellows. The unifying research focus is on stem cell-based models of pre-and postnatal development.

application_id: 11244663
activity_code: F32
title: Developing novel ALK targeting therapies through the expansion of targeted protein degradation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
 This proposal addresses the challenge of resistance to tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) in anaplastic
lymphoma kinase (ALK)-driven neuroblastoma by exploring novel approaches in targeted protein degradation.
Focusing on leveraging endoplasmic reticulum-associated degradation (ERAD) machinery, the research will
identify selective protein degraders to effectively target ALK mutants and overcome resistance mutations,
thereby limiting relapse and improving patient outcomes. Additionally, the project seeks to expand the scope of
targeted degradation therapies by developing new genetic tools and screening approaches, ultimately
broadening the therapeutic toolkit for ALK-driven cancers and beyond.

title: Determining the Molecular Arrangement of GABABR-GIRK Signaling Cascades
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative
Inhibitory neuronal signaling from drugs binding GPCRs often results in GIRK channel activation by Gi/o
heterotrimeric G proteins. Understanding how GABAB receptors activate G proteins which in turn activate
GIRKs is of critical importance to understanding drugs of abuse involving inhibitory signaling. We propose a
large, preformed complex between receptors, GIRK channels, and heterotrimeric G proteins that allows
efficient activation and deactivation neurons, and this project will define this inhibitory signaling complex.

application_id: 11251925
activity_code: K23
title: A Multi-Method Study of Momentary Distress Intolerance and Combustible Cigarette Smoking
org_name: RUTGERS, THE STATE UNIV OF N.J.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Distress intolerance (DI), or one’s inability to withstand difficult experiences, physically and emotionally, is a
risk factor believed to underlie the co-occurrence of cigarette smoking and emotional distress/disorders. In this
mentored research development application, we explore DI as a dynamic variable that can change moment-to-
moment as the result of various internal and external factors. Through the proposed multi-method study, our
goal is to understand these factors that influence DI and subsequent smoking in daily life in order to inform
future interventions that can help people reduce DI in the moments when they are most likely to smoke.

application_id: 11318001
activity_code: F32
title: Parallel training in human and animal studies to evaluate the effects of age-related changes to extended high frequency sensitivity and cognition on higher-order auditory processing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Degraded speech-in-noise performance has been associated with both elevated extended high frequency
hearing thresholds and cognitive decline. Measurement of the extended high frequencies may provide clinical
value in relation to speech perception in the presence of background noise and has not been studied in reference
to the correlation between hearing loss and cognitive decline. Our proposed research involves parallel human
and animal model studies evaluating the role of extended high frequency hearing, speech-in-noise performance,
and biomarker changes for cognitive decline.

org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Precision nutrition approaches aim to move away from a one-size fits all approach to identify individual-level dietary and nutritional intake for optimal health by accounting for individual variability in genes, phenotype, environment, and lifestyle for each person (NIH) – i.e., tailoring nutrition interventions and/or recommendations to individuals by accounting for the complex nutritional ecology reflecting the interaction of a complex system. application_id: 11163911
activity_code: F32
title: Multiparametric characterization of periodontal tissues for inflammation monitoring using quantitative ultrasound, texture analysis and machine learning
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
Periodontal diseases, affecting 42% of adults in the U.S., impose large economic cost, added pain and
systematic health issues on patients that negatively impact their quality of life. The standard diagnostic method
for these inflammatory diseases is currently bleeding on probing, which is invasive, only semi-quantitative, and
subjective. This study aims at developing a non-invasive and objectively quantitative biomarker based on
ultrasonography and a machine learning classifier for periodontal inflammation monitoring by use of an
overarching multiparametric investigation.

title: Investigating the Role of Histamine and HRH1 in Mucus Secretion in the Salivary Glands
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: Project Narrative:
Salivary glands harbor the histamine receptor HRH1; but little is known about the role of histamine and HRH1
signaling in salivary gland mucus secretion and maintenance of oral health. This proposal aims to elucidate the
role of histamine in stimulating mucus secretion and to evaluate the effects of histamine-producing probiotics on
salivary glands. This work has the potential to identify new targets for therapeutics to improve oral health.

title: The role of hnRNPK in pancreatic beta cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
The development of hyperglycemia, insulin insufficiency, and beta cell failure all underly the progression to
Type 2 diabetes. Many efforts have been made to improve the lives of those living with T2D including novel
drugs that enhance insulin secretion and induce weight loss but prevalence of this disease is still climbing.

underlying T2D pathology to help lessen the negative impact this disease has on the world.

application_id: 11241491
activity_code: F32
title: HIF regulation of Ammonia Processing and Fibrosis in MASLD/MASH
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Metabolic associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) and the histologic subtype of active inflammation and
fibrosis (metabolic associated steatohepatitis, MASH) is a chronic medical condition that has a prevalence of
approximately 25% in US Adults, with similar prevalence in other countries. The goal of this study is to
understand how hypoxia drives steatosis and fibrosis through dysregulation of hepatocyte metabolism and
urea cycle dysregulation. These studies will identify targets for clinical intervention.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Precision Nutrition Training Program
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Precision nutrition approaches aim to move away from a one-size fits all approach to identify individual-level
dietary and nutritional intake for optimal health by accounting for individual variability in genes, phenotype,
environment, and lifestyle for each person (NIH) – i.e., tailoring nutrition interventions and/or recommendations

The 2020-30 Strategic Plan for NIH Nutrition Research, developed by the NIH Nutrition Research Task Force,
identifies a key need to produce a nutrition workforce that is trained in Artificial Intelligence (AI) and machine
learning (ML) to solve the most pressing problems in nutrition and health, by applying novel methods and

application_id: 11312388
activity_code: F31
title: Modulation of central vestibular processing for locomotor control
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative
 Balance dysfunction is widespread, can potentially lead to serious injury, and can result from issues
processing motion signals in the brainstem. Proper balance requires accurately predicting how we will move
when we try to walk, but the underlying neural processes, common across vertebrate locomotion, are largely
mysterious. Here we image neural activity in transparent fish to investigate how encoding of head motion in the
brainstem is shaped by expectation during swimming.

phr: Project Narrative The Stanford Genome Training Program trains PhD students and postdoctoral fellows to advance to productive technical and high-level leadership positions in the biomedical sciences, in academia and in the private sector. application_id: 11313157
activity_code: F31
title: TDP-43 dependent cryptic exons in neurodegenerative disorders: pathogenic mechanism and therapeutic strategy
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 TDP-43 is a highly conserved nuclear RNA binding protein that serves a crucial role in RNA regulation
and metabolism. The nuclear clearance of TDP-43 has been implicated in many neurodegenerative diseases,
however very little is known about the loss of TDP-43 in glial cells despite playing a role in the progression of
these diseases. This proposal explores how the loss of TDP-43 in glial cells alter their function and whether
TDP-43 loss-of-function can be targeted therapeutically using a novel splicing repressor.

title: Training Grant in Genomic Analysis and Interpretation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Narrative
Genomics is a major driving force for modern biomedical research. Given the recent advances in genomic
technologies and electronic health records, analysis and interpretation are the biggest bottlenecks to
understanding the basis of human variation and disease. This training program will produce a diverse
generation of computational genomicists with the statistics, computer science, bioinformatics, molecular
biology, and genetics skills necessary to turn this flood of data into biomedical knowledge that can advance the
understanding, diagnosis, and treatment of disease.

application_id: 11244490
activity_code: F32
title: End-Stage Kidney Disease in California's Central Valley: Investigating a Hotspot of Kidney Disease
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
End-stage kidney disease is disproportionately prevalent in California's Central Valley, and the potential links
between heat exposure, agricultural work, and other environmental factors in the region remain unclear. To
address this, we propose investigating this local kidney disease hotspot by evaluating the association between
residential heat exposure and the development of end-stage kidney disease. Concurrently, we will conduct a
study within Central Valley dialysis units to assess patients' lifelong occupational and environmental exposures,
and test whether these are associated with unexplained end-stage kidney disease.

application_id: 11144838
activity_code: F32
title: Sociodemographic and geospatial disparities in outcomes for patients with biliary atresia: A comprehensive analysis using national and state datasets
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
To advance health equity for children diagnosed with biliary atresia, the leading indication for pediatric liver
transplant, a more comprehensive understanding of how race/ethnicity, socioeconomics, and geographic factors
influence outcomes at different time points along the disease continuum is critically needed. This proposal will
analyze both national and statewide datasets and examine how an infant’s racialization and sociodemographic
milieu influences disease-specific outcomes, including access to specialized care, post-surgical complications,
and timing of liver transplant. Our answers will highlight where targeted interventions should be directed to better
support this vulnerable population.

application_id: 11240483
activity_code: F32
title: Using real world evidence to address gaps in the use of estrogen and progesterone therapy in adolescents and premenopausal women with chronic kidney disease
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Project Narrative
Chronic kidney disease (CKD) affects ovarian hormone balance in women, often causing irregular periods and
low estrogen levels, which can weaken bones and increase fracture risk. Estrogen and progesterone (E/P)
therapy may help improve menstrual/reproductive and bone health, but there are significant concerns about their
associated thromboembolic risks. This study will evaluate the impact of E/P therapy on fracture and
thromboembolic risk in adolescents and premenopausal women with CKD, guiding effective and safer care to
support long-term ovarian and skeletal health.

application_id: 11240655
activity_code: F32
title: Deciphering how directional cues are relayed into polarized collective cell migration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
Collective cell migration drives tissue morphogenesis and organ homeostasis in a vast diversity of animal
species, yet we understand little about how epithelial cells sense and interpret the directional cues guiding
migration events. The proposed study will establish a tractable, vertebrate model of epithelial collective cell
migration, the developing zebrafish pronephros (embryonic kidney), to parse the cellular mechanisms guiding
collective migration in a living, developing organ. Long term, understanding the molecular relay guiding
collective polarization and migration of epithelial cells will be key in the treatment of developmental diseases
and in the engineering of tissues and organs.

application_id: 10818330
activity_code: F31
title: Determining the role of AIRE and AIRE-expressing tumor associated macrophages in tumor growth and immunity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE AIRE is critical for maintaining central and peripheral tolerance to prevent autoimmune diseases but can also play a detrimental role in inhibiting immune responses against cancer. While deletion of thymic AIRE expression has been shown to improve immunotherapy response in certain cancers by allowing the development of autoreactive T cells, whether AIRE expression outside of the thymus is involved in regulating anti-tumor immune responses remains unknown. This proposal investigates the role of peripheral AIRE expression and AIRE-expressing tumor-associated macrophages in tumor progression and immunity with the ultimate goal of improving immunotherapy approaches for cancer treatment.

application_id: 11241299
activity_code: F32
title: The role of sympathetic signaling in inflammation-induced colon dysfunction and recovery
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is a chronic gastrointestinal disorder affecting approximately 3 million Americans and
characterized by recurring inflammation often worsened by stress. Despite periods of remission, symptoms such as
dysmotility, abdominal pain, and autonomic dysfunction persist, indicating lasting alterations in gut-brain neural circuits.
This proposal explores the role of sensory-sympathetic pathways in colitis outcomes, including dysmotility and mucosal
healing, to uncover potential therapeutic targets for IBD.

application_id: 11242592
activity_code: F32
title: Alterations of Branched Chain Amino Acid (BCAA) Catabolism in Acute Kidney Injury and Fibrosis
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY NEW YORK STONY BROOK
phr: Narrative
Proximal tubules are the most susceptible cells in the setting of acute kidney injury (AKI), and undergo
fundamental alterations in cellular metabolism such as fatty acid oxidation and branched chain amino acid
(BCAA) catabolism; however, sex-specific differences in PT metabolism are understudied. We recently showed
that activation of BCAA catabolism prevented nephrotoxic AKI in male mice, and that female AKI patients also
have transcriptional dysregulation of BCAA catabolism; however, the enzymes affected differ between males and
females. Therefore, I will elucidate the differentially dysregulated metabolic pathways and their downstream
consequences in male versus female mice and test the utility of activation of BCAA catabolism in treating AKI
and transition to fibrosis.

application_id: 11243807
activity_code: F32
title: Representation and computation of semantic categories in the dorsal visual stream
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Humans and other primates understand and interact with the visual world by breaking it into parts: we detect
discrete objects and group them into discrete categories in myriad ways. In this project we use a novel large-
scale neurophysiology dataset, state-of-the-art neuronal population analyses, and artificial neural network
modelling to study how the brain assembles representations of experience-dependent ‘semantic’ categories
through successive stages of the dorsal visual stream. Understanding the format and function of semantic
category representations in the brain will shed light more broadly on how neural systems may generate
symbolic representations, which is thought to enable our ability to generalize our knowledge and reason
intelligently.

title: Non-canonical roles for ATM kinase in regulating mitochondrial function and redox homeostasis
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE. Ataxia telangiectasia (A-T) is a genetic disorder characterized by cerebellar neurodegeneration, progressive pulmonary dysfunction, immunodeficiencies, insulin resistance, sterility, and cancer predisposition. Data from the Kastan lab suggests that ATM, the gene which is mutated in A-T, plays an essential role in regulating metabolic function, mitochondrial quality control, and redox homeostasis through interactions with the autophagy regulator Beclin-1 and novel, as of yet uncharacterized cytoplasmic proteins. The experiments outlined in this proposal will explore validate these interactions and explore their functional impacts on mitochondrial, metabolic, and redox homeostasis.

application_id: 11069233
activity_code: F32
title: Dynamic Modeling of Dicer Function
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative
The RNase III enzyme Dicer is a highly dynamic and multifunctional protein that plays an important role in the
maturation and regulation of small non-coding RNAs. Unfortunately, current approaches to study the
conformational changes and molecular interactions associated with Dicer function have been unable to answer
key questions regarding Dicer’s mechanism(s) of action related to substrate specificity and helicase activity. In
response, this project will focus on developing a new approach that involves the use of data-informed predictive
simulations, which combine physics-based and knowledge-based simulations with 3D animation software, to
build integrative dynamic models of Dicer function with the goal of answering these mechanistic questions.

activity_code: T32
title: Translational Pulmonary Vascular Biology Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
A large number of human diseases can affect the structure and function of blood vessels in the lung. In fact,
when diseases are complicated by increases in pressure in the lung blood vessels (lung vascular disease),
significant increases in morbidity and mortality are observed, which are often due to dysfunction or failure of the
right heart. This program is directed at training individuals to better understand the causes of lung vascular and
right heart disease with the long-term goal that these investigators will develop new therapies to decrease or
ameliorate the health burden of lung vascular problems.

application_id: 11244317
activity_code: F32
title: A concise dearomative approach to the synthesis of quinolizidine natural products
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
Fused piperidine structures, such as quinolizidine and indolizidine motifs, feature prominently in small-molecule
therapeutic targets with neuroprotective, anti-protozoal, and anti-cancer properties—all of which serve
immediate public health and biomedical priorities. However, even relatively simple fused scaffolds can require
laborious synthetic work to prepare, hampering development in this area. This proposal outlines the high-
throughput, data-driven development and application of asymmetric 3-component Pd cross-coupling reactions
for the rapid assembly of fused alkaloid skeletons, including for the total syntheses of lycopodine and
lycoposerramine H—natural products with potential neuroprotective properties.

The goal of the Hematology Research Training Program is to identify, train and mentor a diverse group of outstanding men and women and to help them become teachers, scholars, and leaders of biomedical research in academia, industry with an emphasis on fields of endeavor that are applicable to the broad range of hematology.

activity_code: F32
title: De novo design of artificial haloperoxidase enzymes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative
Proteins use metal ions to catalyze myriad important reactions with relevance to human health and the
environment. Understanding the detailed interactions between three-dimensional protein structure, metal
ions, and catalytic function, promises improved understanding of diseases and routes to new drugs and
treatments. This work will investigate metal-protein structure-function relationships by designing artificial
metalloenzymes that catalyze haloperoxidation reactions.

phr: NARRATIVE
The burden of cardiovascular disease (CVD) in the United States (US) is steadily increasing, in part due to the
aging of the US population, increased prevalence of conditions such as diabetes, chronic kidney disease and
the metabolic syndrome and the emergence of high-risk populations, i.e., racial/ethnic minorities such as
African-Americans and Hispanics, and persons infected with HIV/AIDS. These increasing health needs
underscore the importance of training basic scientists, clinical scientists and population scientists in an
environment conducive to multidisciplinary team research in order to test more effective approaches to the
prevention and treatment of hypertension and related vascular disease and other comorbidities. This Training
Program addresses these goals, and is the...

Project Narrative Hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin (HPFH) mutations reduce the severity of β-hemoglobinopathies and can be introduced experimentally and for therapeutic benefit. phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This Stimulating Access to Research in Residency program is designed to provide residents from diverse clinical backgrounds with the tools, mentorship, and support necessary to develop promising careers in aging research. title: Circuits, Serotonergic Neurons, and the Modulation of Behavior: Characterization of a Specialized Serotonergic Neuron Subtype Responsive to Dopamine and Central to Social Behavior
org_name: SALK INSTITUTE FOR BIOLOGICAL STUDIES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The serotonergic (5-HT) and dopaminergic neuromodulatory systems have both been implicated in aggression, yet the specific cell types and circuitry involved remain unknown. This project will study a dopamine (DA)- responsive serotonergic neuron subtype involved in the modulation of aggression and acoustic startle reactivity in mice and will inform on the functional interplay between 5-HT and DA in the modulation of behavior. Understanding the circuit structure of this 5-HT neuron subtype may inform better therapeutics for the treatment of escalated aggression and/or altered startle reactivity, which can occur in neuropsychiatric disorders such as intermittent explosive disorder, schizophrenia, autism, and post-traumatic stress disorder.

title: Cellular engineering to improve the efficacy and specificity of targeted immunotherapy
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative Engineered T cells programmed to recognize and kill tumors have emerged as an extraordinarily powerful tool for combating cancer. Nonetheless, aside from chimeric antigen receptor T cells for the treatment of blood cancers, most engineered cell therapies are often ineffective and potentially highly toxic, particularly those that target solid tumors. This research proposal develops synthetic biology tools to combat three major challenges of engineered T cells: tumor recognition, toxicity and the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment.

application_id: 11065448
activity_code: K00
title: Injectable drug-delivery system to repair the blood-brain-barrier after ischemic stroke
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: The thesis and F99 phase of this proposal investigates a nanomedicine-based approach to drive the peripheral nerve regeneration and functional recovery, while also working to limit neuromuscular degeneration resulting from the loss of nerve function by creating new neurons within the muscle to promote and preserve healthier state. The applications of genetic and cell-based therapies extend well beyond the study of nerve regeneration and will be applied to neurodevelopmental disorders in the K00 period. application_id: 11170611
activity_code: DP2
title: Deciphering the immunogenicity of cell death using systematic genetic tools
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
The core function of immunity is to distinguish self from non-self, yet in the process of sterile immunogenic cell
death, our immune system recognizes our own dead cells as foreign. This process can thus lead to autoimmunity,
though it can also be useful in cancer treatment if induced in the right time and place. This proposal seeks to
apply breakthrough functional genomic technologies to interrogate the molecular basis of immunogenic cell
death and potentiate new treatments in autoimmunity, cancer, and other diseases of the immune system.

application_id: 10926916
activity_code: K00
title: Improving the Generalizability of Deep Neural Networks by Teaching Single Nucleotide Polymorphisms Associated with LDCT Features
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Standard diagnostic practice for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) focuses on nodular characteristics such as diameter and growth rate over sequential scans, thus ignoring important diagnostic features in the surrounding parenchyma such as emphysema and chronic inflammation that are associated with an increased risk of a nodule being malignant. Understanding the key parenchymal and tumor-specific features together with other biologic traits that determine whether a nodule is malignant or benign will improve early diagnosis of NSCLC. We will develop convolutional neural networks (CNNs) that utilize these features and traits to provide improved diagnostic accuracy of indeterminate pulmonary nodules seen in low-dose computed tomography images.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative Neuropathic pain is a debilitating form of chronic pain afflicting millions of patients worldwide but is poorly responsive to current analgesic therapies. It is hypothesized that increased excitatory/decreased inhibitory signaling in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord results in the initiation, maintenance, and progression of neuropathic pain. This study uses in vivo intraspinal optogenetics, conditional genetic knockout lines combined with behavioral pharmacology, and spinal cord slice electrophysiology to ultimately determine if peripheral nerve injury increases the excitability of excitatory dorsal horn interneurons (those that contain the type 1 receptor for neuropeptide Y) to contribute to the behavioral manifestations of neuropathic pain.

title: Parallel phenotyping to dissect genetic determinants of bacterial strain diversity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mycobacterium avium is an environmental bacterium that is capable of infecting humans and causing serious disease, requiring months to years of antibiotic treatment to cure. application_id: 11014626
activity_code: DP2
title: A data-driven approach towards generation of permeable peptide therapeutics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OREGON
phr: Project Narrative
 A large portion of potential drug targets are inaccessible to the most widely used therapeutic modalities:
small molecules and antibodies. Peptides are powerful alternatives for drugging these targets; however, their
use has been hugely underexplored due to challenges in the way of obtaining cell-permeable peptides that can
access intracellular targets. In this proposal, we use a novel data-driven approach that combines
state-of-the-art computational techniques, such as peptide design and machine learning, with experimental
data generation to develop methods and datasets that will pave the way for the development of next
generation cell-permeable peptide therapeutics.

application_id: 10930079
activity_code: U54
title: Genomic Workforce Development Core
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF HAWAII AT MANOA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There is an urgent national need to grow a strong genomic workforce which includes populations underrepresented in biomedical research. We propose a streamlined educational feeder pipeline called PoWER-G (Promotion of Workforce Employment and Readiness in Genomics) providing an innovative “on-the- degree” training and professional development platform which draws upon the substantial student diversity within the University of Hawaii system. Knowledge generated by this pilot project will lead to development of a formal 1-year Graduate Certificate Program to promote diversity in the genomic workforce and to continue to meet the exponential growth in genomics.

application_id: 11009676
activity_code: DP2
title: The cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying nociception and pain
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pain is a fundamental sensation that is required for alerting organisms to persistent or acute environmental
dangers. In this project, we will focus on understanding how the diverse group of neurons, known as
nociceptors, are able to initially detect and transmit noxious stimuli from peripheral organs, such as the skin.
We will use molecular genetics and functional imaging approaches to perform a cellular and molecular
analysis to understand the structure and function of each fundamentally distinct nociceptor subtype.

title: Programmable Off-the-Shelf Dendritic Cells as an Immunotherapy Discovery Platform
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative 
 
This proposal will expand access to cell immunotherapies for more people. We will build programmable iPSC-­
derived  Dendritic  cells  as  a  way  to  identify  new  T  cell  receptor  immunotherapies  for  cancers  and  infectious

targets.

application_id: 11134462
activity_code: U45
title: FY25-FY29 NIEHS WTP
org_name: SUSTAINABLE WORKPLACE ALLIANCE, INC.
phr: The Sustainable Workplace Alliance and its alliance partners will provide model training to better protect workers, first responders and citizens that have been negatively impacted by pollution and emergency events.  These populations are unlikely to know the inherent risks of the work they do, the hazards created by pollution or hazardous materials, and the most effective ways to protect themselves from these harms.  This model training and outreach program is designed to reduce injuries and death in environmental, remediation, construction and emergency responder fields of work including those that work in these fields in a volunteer capacity.

application_id: 11014663
activity_code: DP2
title: Decode Mitochondrial Morphology Dynamics to Predict Cell Fate Decisions
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative
PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE
Abnormalities in renewing the outer cell layers of the intestine lead to disorders of the gastrointestinal tract such
as inflammatory bowel diseases and colorectal cancer (respectively ~3 million and ~140 thousand US cases
annually) . A change in the morphology of the mitochondrial network is a critical step in this renewal process.
The aim of this proposal is to understand these mitochondrial network morphology changes and how they affect
the fate of intestinal stem cells during differentiation to develop new therapies for these devastating diseases.

activity_code: DP2
title: Algorithms and Software for Biomolecular Structure Determination at the Proteome Scale
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
 Structural biology provides invaluable insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying health and
disease, but its low-throughput nature limits the speed and scope of discoveries. This research aims to
develop AI algorithms and software that leverage machine learning to enable biomolecular structure
determination at the proteome scale. By enabling high-resolution visualization of complex protein structures
across entire proteomes, this work will advance our understanding of cellular mechanisms and contribute to
new approaches in drug discovery and disease treatment.

activity_code: DP2
title: Defining the role of single-cell heterogeneity in bacterial vaginosis
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite extensive characterization, we struggle to understand and control the progression of human-associated microbial communities, including bacterial vaginosis (BV), a condition that is directly impactful to women’s health and the acquisition of sexually transmitted infections. This proposal investigates single-cell genomic and transcriptomic heterogeneity as two underexplored areas that impact the progression of BV. This intellectually and methodologically innovative research will transform our understanding of the development, treatment, and recurrence of BV and will provide broad-reaching insights into new strategies to prevent and treat polymicrobial infections.

application_id: 11170662
activity_code: DP2
title: Defining HIV evolution at the innate immune interface
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 HIV evolves to escape the human immune system and drug therapies, but it is constrained by the need to
interface with host cell components that allow for infection. This proposal will take an innovative high-
throughput approach to comprehensively parse the functional and innate immune-imposed evolutionary
constraints on the HIV capsid protein, a critical immune and therapeutic target. These results will reveal how
HIV structurally interfaces with and escapes from human innate immunity, and they will allow us to identify viral
Achille's heels that can be synergistically constrained by native human immunity and capsid-targeting drugs to
limit viral escape, which is a major barrier to effective antiviral therapy.

application_id: 10973705
activity_code: K00
title: From Epileptogenesis to Cognitive Dysfunction: The Role of Immature Astroglia in MTLE
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS SAN ANTONIO
phr: Project Narrative
Individuals with epilepsy caused by hyperactive mTOR signaling and potentiated by a concurrent impairment in
cerebellar function are often treatment resistant when treated with traditional anti-epileptic drugs. Treatments
that target the underlying mechanisms have been found to reduce the occurrence of seizures but have been
associated with severe to life-threatening side effects. In addition to providing the expertise and training for a
career as tenured-track research faculty, here, the current proposal aims to determine whether vitamin D, a
well-tolerable therapy with mTOR inhibiting effects, can serve as a novel or adjunctive therapy in the treatment
of hyperactive mTOR induced epilepsies and co-morbid disorders.

title: Unobtrusive and continuous monitoring of cognitive changes using smartphones
org_name: NORTHEASTERN UNIVERSITY

To facilitate successful aging, we need effective methods for early detection of neurodegeneration and for monitoring cognitive function. application_id: 11135519
activity_code: DP2
title: Acceptance of non-self: Decoding intestinal immune tolerance during early life
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative
The intestinal immune system has a unique ability to discriminate harmless commensal microbes from
pathogens, yet remarkably little is known about the cell types that initiate tolerance to commensal antigens at
the time of colonization in early life. A developmental wave of antigen-presenting cells (APCs) seeds the
intestine during the first week of life. This proposal seeks to determine how these APCs establish tolerance to
commensal microbiota, the tissue signals that drive their development, and their role in susceptibility to
intestinal immune mediated diseases in adulthood.

application_id: 10764215
activity_code: DP2
title: Engineering synthetic cellular crosstalk for transplantation tolerance
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Acute and chronic alloimmune reactions against allografts are the major causes of transplantation rejection. Developing safe and effective immune suppressive treatments to achieve long-term transplantation tolerance has been difficult because we don’t have strategies to specifically intervene with multiple components of the alloimmune reactions without compromising the entire host immune system. Here we will develop an orthogonal vaccine to synthetically bridge the crosstalk between antigen presenting cells and engineered regulatory T cells to simultaneously and specifically suppress alloimmune reactions at both the effector site (allograft) and the priming site (allograft draining lymphoid tissue) for long-term transplantation tolerance.

title: Identifying new drivers of ovarian cancer from the non-coding genome by converging germline risk variants and somatic mutations
org_name: CEDARS-SINAI MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Ovarian cancer is responsible for more than 150,000 deaths per year and so it remains a high priority to develop effective prevention approaches and to identify new disease specific therapies to reduce disease mortality. The research proposed here has two components: (1) To characterize the genetic component in ovarian cancer development through epigenomic landscape; (2) to establish the functional relevance of genetic findings for clinical translation. This is a highly integrated multi-disciplinary research proposal, and these approaches to novel discovery and mechanistic understanding are desperately needed to advance our basic knowledge of this disease to develop clinical interventions that reduce mortality.

activity_code: K00
title: Ventral Pallidum Circuits in Motivation, Risky Decision Making, and Opioid Addiction
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Drugs of abuse, like opioids, hijack brain circuits that govern adaptive motivation and decision making, yet the identity and function of such circuits remains largely unresolved. Gaining a deeper understanding of how such circuits generate both healthy and unhealthy motivation will greatly enhance our ability to target and treat those brain regions rendered pathological in psychiatric disorders like addiction. Here, I use gene transfer approaches and circuit manipulations to determine functions of the ventral pallidum in risky decision making, and seeking of addictive opioid drugs.

from fetal life to early childhood, and 4) characterization of ex vivo epigenetic and transcriptomic assessments
